(commonly known as **Ford**, sometimes abbreviated as **FoMoCo**) is an American [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") automobile manufacturer headquartered in [Dearborn, Michigan](/wiki/Dearborn,_Michigan "Dearborn, Michigan"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It was founded by [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford") and incorporated on June 16, 1903. The company sells automobiles and commercial vehicles under the [Ford](/wiki/List_of_Ford_vehicles "List of Ford vehicles") brand, and luxury cars under its [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_Motor_Company "Lincoln Motor Company") brand. The company is listed on the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") under the single-letter [ticker symbol](/wiki/Ticker_symbol "Ticker symbol") F, and is controlled by the [Ford family](/wiki/Ford_family_(Michigan) "Ford family (Michigan)"). They have minority ownership, but a plurality of the voting power.[[3]](#cite_note-FordFamily-3)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The **Ford Motor Company** (commonly known as **Ford**, sometimes abbreviated as **FoMoCo**) is an American [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") automobile manufacturer headquartered in [Dearborn, Michigan](/wiki/Dearborn,_Michigan "Dearborn, Michigan"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It was founded by [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford") and incorporated on June 16, 1903. The company sells automobiles and commercial vehicles under the [Ford](/wiki/List_of_Ford_vehicles "List of Ford vehicles") brand, and luxury cars under its [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_Motor_Company "Lincoln Motor Company") brand. The company is listed on the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") under the single-letter [ticker symbol](/wiki/Ticker_symbol "Ticker symbol") F, and is controlled by the [Ford family](/wiki/Ford_family_(Michigan) "Ford family (Michigan)"). They have minority ownership, but a plurality of the voting power.[[3]](#cite_note-FordFamily-3)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Ford introduced methods for large-scale manufacturing of cars and large-scale management of an industrial workforce using elaborately engineered manufacturing sequences typified by moving [assembly lines](/wiki/Assembly_line "Assembly line"). By 1914, these methods were known around the world, simply known as [Fordism](/wiki/Fordism "Fordism"). Ford's former British subsidiaries [Jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") and [Land Rover](/wiki/Land_Rover "Land Rover"), acquired in 1989 and 2000, respectively. They were sold to the Indian automaker [Tata Motors](/wiki/Tata_Motors "Tata Motors") in March 2008. Ford owned the Swedish automaker [Volvo](/wiki/Volvo_Cars "Volvo Cars") from 1999 to 2010.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In the third quarter of 2010, Ford discontinued the [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(automobile) "Mercury (automobile)") brand, under which it had marketed upscale cars in the United States, Canada, Mexico, and the Middle East since 1938.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Ford is the second-largest American-based automaker, behind [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), and the [sixth-largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_automotive_manufacturers_by_production "List of automotive manufacturers by production"), with the biggest, being [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota"), the [Volkswagen Group](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group"), [Hyundai](/wiki/Hyundai_Motor_Group "Hyundai Motor Group"), [Stellantis](/wiki/Stellantis "Stellantis"), and General Motors, based on 2022 vehicle production.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The company went public in 1956 but the Ford family, through special [Class B shares](/wiki/Preferred_stock#Other_features_or_rights "Preferred stock"), retain 40 percent of the voting rights.[[3]](#cite_note-FordFamily-3)[[9]](#cite_note-9) During the [2008–2010 automotive industry crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932010_automotive_industry_crisis "2008–2010 automotive industry crisis"), the company struggled financially but did not have to be rescued by the federal government, unlike the other two major US automakers.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Ford Motors has since returned to profitability,[[12]](#cite_note-12) and was the eleventh-ranked overall American-based company in the 2018 [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") list, based on global revenues in 2017 of $156.7 billion.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2023, Ford produced 4.4 million automobiles, and employed about 177,000 employees worldwide. The company operates joint ventures in China ([Changan Ford](/wiki/Changan_Ford "Changan Ford") and [Jiangling Ford](/wiki/Jiangling_Ford "Jiangling Ford")), Taiwan ([Ford Lio Ho](/wiki/Ford_Lio_Ho "Ford Lio Ho")), Thailand ([AutoAlliance Thailand](/wiki/AutoAlliance_Thailand "AutoAlliance Thailand")), and Turkey ([Ford Otosan](/wiki/Ford_Otosan "Ford Otosan")). Ford owns a 32% stake in China's [Jiangling Motors](/wiki/Jiangling_Motors "Jiangling Motors").[[14]](#cite_note-AR-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## History

Main article: [History of Ford Motor Company](/wiki/History_of_Ford_Motor_Company "History of Ford Motor Company")

### 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Henry_ford_1919.jpg/250px-Henry_ford_1919.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_ford_1919.jpg)

Founder [Henry Ford](/wiki/Henry_Ford "Henry Ford"), 1919

The [Henry Ford Company](/wiki/Henry_Ford_Company "Henry Ford Company") was Henry Ford's first attempt at a car manufacturing company and was established on November 3, 1901. This became the [Cadillac Motor Company](/wiki/Cadillac_Motor_Company "Cadillac Motor Company") on August 22, 1902, after Ford left with the rights to his name.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1903, the Ford Motor Company was launched in a converted factory, with $28,000, equivalent to $1,003,000 in 2025, in cash from twelve investors, most notably [John](/wiki/John_Francis_Dodge "John Francis Dodge") and [Horace Dodge](/wiki/Horace_Elgin_Dodge "Horace Elgin Dodge"), who later founded the [Dodge Brothers Motor Vehicle Company](/wiki/Dodge_Brothers_Motor_Vehicle_Company "Dodge Brothers Motor Vehicle Company").

The first president was not Ford, but local banker [John S. Gray](/wiki/John_S._Gray_(Michigan) "John S. Gray (Michigan)"), who was chosen in order to assuage investors' fears that Ford would leave the new company the way he had left its predecessor. During its early years, the company produced just a few cars a day at [its factory on Mack Avenue](/wiki/Ford_Mack_Avenue_Plant "Ford Mack Avenue Plant") and later at its [factory on Piquette Avenue](/wiki/Ford_Piquette_Avenue_Plant "Ford Piquette Avenue Plant") in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), Michigan. Groups of two or three men worked on each car, assembling it from parts made mostly by supplier companies contracting for Ford. Within a decade the company led the world in the expansion and refinement of the [assembly line](/wiki/Assembly_line "Assembly line") concept, and Ford soon brought much of the part production in-house, via [vertical integration](/wiki/Vertical_integration "Vertical integration").

Henry Ford was 39 years old when he founded the Ford Motor Company, which became one of the world's largest and most profitable companies. It has been in continuous family control for over 100 years, and is one of the largest family-controlled companies in the world.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The first gasoline-powered [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") was created in 1885 by the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") inventor [Karl Benz](/wiki/Karl_Benz "Karl Benz"), with his [Benz Patent-Motorwagen](/wiki/Benz_Patent-Motorwagen "Benz Patent-Motorwagen"). More efficient production methods were needed to make automobiles affordable for [middle class](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") people; Ford contributed to this effort through a variety of innovations, including the introduction of the first moving [assembly line](/wiki/Assembly_line "Assembly line") in 1913 at the Ford [factory in Highland Park](/wiki/Highland_Park_Ford_Plant "Highland Park Ford Plant").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/1910Ford-T.jpg/250px-1910Ford-T.jpg)](/wiki/File:1910Ford-T.jpg)

A 1910 [Model T](/wiki/Model_T "Model T"), introduced in 1908, photographed in [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City")

Between 1903 and 1908, Ford produced the Models [A](/wiki/Ford_Model_A_(1903-04) "Ford Model A (1903-04)"), B, C, F, K, N, R, and S. Hundreds or a few thousand of most of these were sold per year. In 1908, Ford introduced the mass-produced [Model T](/wiki/Model_T "Model T"), which totaled millions sold over nearly 20 years. In 1927, Ford replaced the T with the [Model A](/wiki/Ford_Model_A_(1927%E2%80%931931) "Ford Model A (1927–1931)"), the first production car with [laminated safety glass](/wiki/Laminated_glass "Laminated glass") in the windshield.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Ford launched the [first low-priced car with a V8](/wiki/Ford_flathead_V8 "Ford flathead V8") engine in 1932.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Ford_Model_AA_truck_displayed_in_Kilgore%2C_Texas_East_Texas_Oil_Museum_%28Brighter%29.jpg/250px-Ford_Model_AA_truck_displayed_in_Kilgore%2C_Texas_East_Texas_Oil_Museum_%28Brighter%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Model_AA_truck_displayed_in_Kilgore,_Texas_East_Texas_Oil_Museum_(Brighter).jpg)

[Ford Model AA](/wiki/Ford_Model_AA "Ford Model AA") truck (based on the Model A) displayed in [Kilgore, Texas](/wiki/Kilgore,_Texas "Kilgore, Texas")

In an attempt to compete with General Motors' mid-priced Pontiac, Oldsmobile, and Buick, Ford created the Mercury in 1939 as a higher-priced companion car to Ford. Henry Ford purchased the Lincoln Motor Company in 1922,[[21]](#cite_note-21) in order to compete with such brands as Cadillac and Packard for the luxury segment of the automobile market.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1929, Ford was contracted by the government of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") to set up the [Gorky Automobile Plant](/wiki/GAZ "GAZ") in Russia initially producing Ford Model A and AAs, thereby playing an important role in the [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") of that country and consequently the Soviet war effort during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[23]](#cite_note-23) To that end, in 1944, Stalin wrote a letter to the [U.S. Chamber of Commerce](/wiki/United_States_Chamber_of_Commerce "United States Chamber of Commerce") stating that Henry Ford was "one of the world's greatest industrialists".[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Ford_Liberators_Willow_Run_1944.jpg/250px-Ford_Liberators_Willow_Run_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Liberators_Willow_Run_1944.jpg)

[B-24 Liberator](/wiki/Consolidated_B-24_Liberator "Consolidated B-24 Liberator") bombers being mass-produced at Ford's [Willow Run assembly plant](/wiki/Willow_Run "Willow Run"), 1944

During World War II, the [United States Department of War](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War") picked Ford to mass-produce the [Consolidated B-24 Liberator](/wiki/Consolidated_B-24_Liberator "Consolidated B-24 Liberator") bomber at its Willow Run assembly plant.[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Ford Werke](/wiki/Ford_Germany "Ford Germany") and [Ford SAF](/wiki/Ford_SAF "Ford SAF"), Ford's subsidiaries in Germany and France, respectively, produced military vehicles and other equipment for [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany")'s [war effort](/wiki/German_re-armament "German re-armament"). Some of Ford's operations in Germany at the time were run [using forced labor](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II").

The creation of a scientific laboratory in Dearborn, Michigan, in 1951, doing unfettered basic research, led to Ford's involvement in [superconductivity](/wiki/Superconductivity "Superconductivity") research. In 1964, Ford Research Labs made a key breakthrough with the invention of a superconducting quantum interference device or [SQUID](/wiki/SQUID "SQUID").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Ford offered [the Lifeguard safety package from 1956](/wiki/Lifeguard_(automobile_safety) "Lifeguard (automobile safety)"), which included such innovations as a standard deep-dish steering wheel, optional front, and, for the first time in a car, rear seatbelts, and an optional padded dash.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Ford introduced child-proof door locks into its products in 1957, and, in the same year, offered the first retractable hardtop on a mass-produced six-seater car.[[28]](#cite_note-Flory_Jr._2008-28)

In late 1955, Ford established the Continental division as a separate luxury car division. This division was responsible for the manufacture and sale of the famous [Continental Mark II](/wiki/Continental_Mark_II "Continental Mark II"). At the same time, the [Edsel](/wiki/Edsel "Edsel") division was created to design and market that car starting with the 1958 model year. Due to limited sales of the Continental and the Edsel disaster, Ford merged Mercury, Edsel, and Lincoln into "M-E-L", which reverted to "Lincoln-Mercury" after Edsel's November 1959 demise.[[28]](#cite_note-Flory_Jr._2008-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Ford_Pavilion.jpg/250px-Ford_Pavilion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Pavilion.jpg)

The introduction of the [Ford Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") at the [1964 New York World's Fair](/wiki/1964_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1964 New York World's Fair").

The [Ford Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") was introduced on April 17, 1964, during the [1964 New York World's Fair](/wiki/1964_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1964 New York World's Fair"), where Ford had a pavilion made by [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) In 1965, Ford introduced the seat belt reminder light.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

With the 1980s, Ford introduced several highly successful vehicles around the world. During the 1980s, Ford began using the advertising slogan, "Have you driven a Ford, lately?" to introduce new customers to their brand and make their vehicles appear more modern. In 1990 and 1994, respectively, Ford also acquired [Jaguar Cars](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") and [Aston Martin](/wiki/Aston_Martin "Aston Martin").[[32]](#cite_note-32) During the mid-to-late 1990s, Ford continued to sell large numbers of vehicles, in a booming [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") economy with a soaring stock market and low fuel prices.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

With the dawn of the new century, legacy [health care costs](/wiki/Health_care_prices_in_the_United_States "Health care prices in the United States"), higher fuel prices, and a faltering economy led to falling market shares, declining sales, and diminished profit margins. Most of the corporate profits came from financing consumer automobile loans through [Ford Motor Credit Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Credit_Company "Ford Motor Credit Company").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Bill_Ford_2011.jpg/250px-Bill_Ford_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_Ford_2011.jpg)

[William Clay Ford Jr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Jr. "William Clay Ford Jr."), great-grandson of Henry Ford, serves as the [executive chairman](/wiki/Executive_chairman "Executive chairman") at the board of Ford Motor Company.

[William Clay Ford Jr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Jr. "William Clay Ford Jr."), great-grandson of Henry Ford (better known as "Bill"), was appointed executive chairman in 1998, and also became chief executive officer of the company in 2001, with the departure of [Jacques Nasser](/wiki/Jacques_Nasser "Jacques Nasser"), becoming the first member of the Ford family to head the company since the retirement of his uncle, [Henry Ford II](/wiki/Henry_Ford_II "Henry Ford II"), in 1982. Ford sold motorsport engineering company [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth") to [Gerald Forsythe](/wiki/Gerald_Forsythe "Gerald Forsythe") and [Kevin Kalkhoven](/wiki/Kevin_Kalkhoven "Kevin Kalkhoven") in 2004, the start of a decrease in Ford's motorsport involvement. Upon the retirement of president and chief operations officer Jim Padilla in April 2006, Bill Ford assumed his roles as well. Five months later, in September, Ford named [Alan Mulally](/wiki/Alan_Mulally "Alan Mulally") as president and CEO, with Ford continuing as executive chairman.

By 2005, both Ford and [GM](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors")'s corporate bonds had been downgraded to junk status[[35]](#cite_note-35) as a result of high U.S. health care costs for an [aging workforce](/wiki/Aging_in_the_American_workforce "Aging in the American workforce"), soaring gasoline prices, eroding market share, and an overdependence on declining [SUV](/wiki/Sport_utility_vehicle "Sport utility vehicle") sales. Profit margins decreased on large vehicles due to increased "incentives" (in the form of rebates or low-interest financing) to offset declining demand.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In the latter half of 2005, Chairman Bill Ford asked newly appointed Ford Americas Division President [Mark Fields](/wiki/Mark_Fields_(businessman) "Mark Fields (businessman)") to develop a plan to return the company to profitability. Fields previewed the plan, named *[The Way Forward](/wiki/The_Way_Forward "The Way Forward")*, at the board meeting of the company on December 7, 2005, and it was unveiled to the public on January 23, 2006. "The Way Forward" included resizing the company to match market realities, dropping some unprofitable and inefficient models, consolidating production lines, closing 14 factories and cutting 30,000 jobs.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Ford moved to introduce a range of new vehicles, including "[Crossover SUVs](/wiki/Crossover_SUV "Crossover SUV")" built on [unibody](/wiki/Unibody "Unibody") car platforms, rather than more [body-on-frame](/wiki/Body-on-frame "Body-on-frame") chassis. In developing the hybrid electric powertrain technologies for the [Ford Escape Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_Escape_Hybrid "Ford Escape Hybrid") SUV, the company licensed similar Toyota hybrid technologies[[38]](#cite_note-38) in order to avoid patent infringements.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Ford announced [*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] that it would team up with electricity supply company [Southern California Edison](/wiki/Southern_California_Edison "Southern California Edison") (SCE) to examine the future of [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") in terms of how home and vehicle energy systems will work with the electrical grid. Under the multimillion-dollar, multi-year project, Ford is to convert a demonstration fleet of [Ford Escape Hybrids](/wiki/Ford_Escape_Hybrid "Ford Escape Hybrid") into plug-in hybrids, and SCE is to evaluate how the vehicles might interact with the home and the utility's electrical grid. Some of the vehicles are to be evaluated "in typical customer settings", according to Ford.[[40]](#cite_note-akhrum-40)[[41]](#cite_note-edison-41)

In December 2006, the company raised its borrowing capacity to about $25 billion, placing substantially all corporate assets as collateral.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Chairman Bill Ford has stated that "bankruptcy is not an option".[[43]](#cite_note-43) Ford and the [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers"), representing approximately 46,000 hourly workers in North America, agreed to a historic contract settlement in November 2007 giving the company a substantial break in terms of its ongoing retiree health care costs and other economic issues. The agreement included the establishment of a company-funded, independently run [Voluntary Employee Beneficiary Association](/wiki/Voluntary_Employee_Beneficiary_Association "Voluntary Employee Beneficiary Association") (VEBA) trust to shift the burden of retiree health care from the company's books, thereby improving its balance sheet. This arrangement took effect on January 1, 2010. As a sign of its currently strong cash position, Ford contributed its entire current liability (estimated at US$5.5 billion as of December 31, 2009) to the VEBA in cash, and also pre-paid US$500 million of its future liabilities to the fund. The agreement also gave hourly workers the job security they were seeking by having the company commit to substantial investments in most of its factories.

The automaker reported the largest annual loss in company history in 2006 of $12.7 billion,[[44]](#cite_note-44) and estimated that it would not return to profitability until 2009.[[45]](#cite_note-45) However, Ford surprised [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") in the second quarter of 2007 by posting a $750 million profit. Despite the gains, the company finished the year with a $2.7 billion loss, largely attributed to finance restructuring at [Volvo](/wiki/Volvo_Cars "Volvo Cars").[[46]](#cite_note-46) On June 2, 2008, Ford sold its Jaguar and Land Rover operations to [Tata Motors](/wiki/Tata_Motors "Tata Motors") for $2.3 billion.[[47]](#cite_note-r_20080602-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Kansas_City_Assembly.png/250px-Kansas_City_Assembly.png)](/wiki/File:Kansas_City_Assembly.png)

A worker installs a seat into a Ford F-150 at the [Ford Kansas City Assembly Plant](/wiki/Ford_Kansas_City_Assembly_Plant "Ford Kansas City Assembly Plant") in 2008 which was a critical time for the automaker due to the [2008–2010 automotive industry crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932010_automotive_industry_crisis "2008–2010 automotive industry crisis")

During congressional hearings held in November 2008 at Washington D.C., Ford's Alan Mulally stated that "We at Ford are hopeful that we have enough liquidity. But we also must prepare ourselves for the prospect of further deteriorating economic conditions". He went on to state that "The collapse of one of our competitors would have a severe impact on Ford" and that Ford Motor Company supported both Chrysler and General Motors in their search for government bridge loans during the [2008–2010 automotive industry crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932010_automotive_industry_crisis "2008–2010 automotive industry crisis").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Together, the three companies presented action plans for the sustainability of the industry. Mulally stated that "In addition to our plan, we are also here today to request support for the industry. In the near-term, Ford does not require access to a government bridge loan. However, we request a credit line of $9 billion as a critical backstop or safeguard against worsening conditions as we drive transformational change in our company".[[51]](#cite_note-51) GM and Chrysler received government loans and financing through [T.A.R.P.](/wiki/T.A.R.P. "T.A.R.P.") legislation funding provisions.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

On December 19, the cost of [credit default swaps](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap") to insure the debt of Ford was 68 percent of the sum insured for five years, in addition to annual payments of 5 percent. That meant $6.8 million paid upfront to insure $10 million in debt, in addition to payments of $500,000 per year.[[53]](#cite_note-53) In January 2009, Ford reported a $14.6 billion loss in the preceding year, a record for the company. The company retained sufficient liquidity to fund its operations. Through April 2009, Ford's strategy of [debt-for-equity](/wiki/Debt-for-equity "Debt-for-equity") exchanges erased $9.9 billion in liabilities (28% of its total) in order to leverage its cash position.[[54]](#cite_note-Dolan-54) These actions yielded Ford a $2.7 billion profit in fiscal year 2009, the company's first full-year profit in four years.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In 2012, Ford's corporate bonds were upgraded from junk to investment grade again, citing sustainable, lasting improvements.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

On October 29, 2012, Ford announced the sale of its climate control components business, its last remaining automotive components operation, to Detroit Thermal Systems LLC for an undisclosed price.[[57]](#cite_note-57) On November 1, 2012, Ford announced that [CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO") Alan Mulally would stay with the company until 2014. Ford also named Mark Fields, its president of operations in the Americas, as its new chief operating officer[[58]](#cite_note-58) Mulally was paid a compensation of over $174 million in his previous seven years at Ford since 2006. The generous amount has been a sore point for some workers of the company.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In April 2016, Ford announced a plan to modernize its Dearborn engineering and headquarters campuses through a ten-year building project. The result would see the number of Ford employees working in these areas doubling, to 24,000. During construction, some 2000 of the employees were relocated out of the campus to a temporary location in a disused section of the local shopping mall.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Facilities would also be altered to allow ride-sharing and electric and self-driving vehicles.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Estimates of the construction cost were $1.2 billion.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Michigan_Central_Train_Station_Exterior_2010.jpg/250px-Michigan_Central_Train_Station_Exterior_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michigan_Central_Train_Station_Exterior_2010.jpg)

The historic, once abandoned [Michigan Central Station](/wiki/Michigan_Central_Station "Michigan Central Station") was purchased by Ford Motor Company in May 2018 and is expected to undergo a significant four-year renovation.

On January 3, 2017, Ford [CEO](/wiki/CEO "CEO") Mark Fields announced that in a "vote of confidence" because of the pro-business climate being fostered in part by [President-elect](/wiki/President-elect_of_the_United_States "President-elect of the United States") [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), Ford had canceled plans to invest $1.6 billion in a new plant in Mexico to manufacture the [Ford Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus "Ford Focus"); instead, the company would invest $700 million in [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), which it planned to use to create 700 new jobs. The Focus would now be manufactured in the existing plant in Mexico.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Also in 2017, Ford began development of a new mixed-use urban campus in [the Corktown neighborhood of Detroit](/wiki/Corktown,_Detroit "Corktown, Detroit"), with its purchase, renovation, and occupation of The Factory at Michigan and Rosa Parks. The new site was expected to have a major focus on the development of [autonomous vehicle](/wiki/Autonomous_vehicle "Autonomous vehicle") and [electric vehicle](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") technology.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Ford later began buying up other parcels of land in Corktown including a very high-profile purchase of [Michigan Central Station](/wiki/Michigan_Central_Station "Michigan Central Station") which is planned to become the hub of their Corktown campus, and the adjacent [Roosevelt Warehouse](/wiki/Roosevelt_Warehouse "Roosevelt Warehouse").[[65]](#cite_note-65) Ford expects to move 2,500 of its employees, roughly 5 percent of its southeast Michigan workforce, to the campus with space for an additional 2,500 entrepreneurs, technology companies and partners.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Bill Ford envisioned the first-floor concourse of the train station to be a public gathering place with retail outlets and restaurants.[[67]](#cite_note-67) In February 2017, Ford Motor Co. acquired majority ownership of [Argo AI](/wiki/Argo_AI "Argo AI"), a self-driving car startup.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In May 2017, Ford announced cuts to its global workforce amid efforts to address the company's declining share price and to improve profits. The company is targeting $3 billion in cost reduction and a nearly 10% reduction in the salaried workforce in Asia and North America to enhance earnings in 2018.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) Jim Hackett was announced to replace Mark Fields as CEO of Ford Motor. Mr. Hackett most recently oversaw the formation of Ford Smart Mobility, a unit responsible for experimenting with car-sharing programs, self-driving ventures and other programs aimed at helping Ford better compete with [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber"), [Alphabet Inc.](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc.") and other tech giants looking to edge in on the auto industry.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

On April 25, 2018, Ford announced that it would discontinue passenger cars in the North American market in the next four years, except for the Mustang, due to declining demand and profitability.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The Focus Active, a crossover SUV based on the newly unveiled fourth-generation Focus, was also intended to be marketed in the United States. Due to the vehicle being manufactured in China, Ford later announced that it would not release the Focus Active in the United States, due to [tariffs imposed by the Trump administration on Chinese exports](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_States_trade_war "China–United States trade war").[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

In March 2020, the Detroit [United Auto Workers](/wiki/United_Auto_Workers "United Auto Workers") union announced that after discussion with the leaders of [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), Ford, and [Fiat Chrysler Automobiles](/wiki/Fiat_Chrysler_Automobiles "Fiat Chrysler Automobiles"), the carmakers would partially shut down factories on a "rotating" basis to mitigate the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[76]](#cite_note-76) On March 24, representatives of Ford announced that production in the US, Canada, and Mexico would not resume on March 30 as originally planned, amid the further coronavirus pandemic spread.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In the first quarter of 2020, Ford's sales dropped by 15%, entailing the loss of $2 billion.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

With the change in the demand for the sport vehicles, on January 6, 2021, Ford reported a sales fall of 9.8% in the fourth quarter, selling 542,749 vehicles, compared to 601,862 in 2019.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In April 2021, Ford said that it would provide COVID-19 vaccines for its employees, who were to obtain them at the company; at the beginning the vaccination program would be in southeast Michigan, Missouri and Ohio, but it was to be expanded later on to other locations.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

In May 2021, Ford announced a battery joint venture, [BlueOval SK](/wiki/BlueOval_SK "BlueOval SK"), with [SK Innovation](/wiki/SK_Innovation "SK Innovation") in North America.[[81]](#cite_note-:02-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

In September 2021, Ford entered a partnership with self-driving startup [Argo AI](/wiki/Argo_AI "Argo AI") and American multinational retail corporation [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart"), and introduced a multi-city service for Walmart customers to have their online orders of groceries delivered to their homes by Ford's self-driving test vehicles with Argo's self-driving system.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Jim-farley-by-bill-cramer.jpg/250px-Jim-farley-by-bill-cramer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jim-farley-by-bill-cramer.jpg)

Jim Farley in 2008

In March 2022, Ford announced that it would restructure the company into three separate divisions. Ford Model E is to focus on electric vehicles; Ford Blue is to focus on internal combustion vehicles; and the existing commercial division is to be rebranded as Ford Pro, to focus on vehicle distribution and service.[[84]](#cite_note-:0-84)[[4]](#cite_note-DFP0302-4) In August 2022, Ford announced it planned layoffs of roughly 3,000 employees and contract workers, confirming earlier reporting. The cuts would mostly affect divisions in the US, Canada, and India, which Jim Farley said would allow the company to prepare for the future of electric, software-heavy vehicles.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In February 2023, Ford announced that it was going to cut 3,800 jobs across Europe, with the job cuts mainly focusing on their German and British workforce. Ford will be cutting 2,300 jobs from Germany, 1,300 from the United Kingdom, and an additional 200 jobs in the rest of Europe, according to the head of Ford Germany, Martin Sander. The cuts will mainly be done to the company's engineers.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Ford also announced during the year that their electric vehicle business had lost $3 billion before taxes over the past two years and will lose a similar amount in 2023 as the company looks to significantly invest in Electric Technology. The Ford Model E is expected to be profitable by 2026.[[88]](#cite_note-:1-88)

In April 2023, United Kingdom ministers approved Ford's [BlueCruise](/w/index.php?title=BlueCruise&action=edit&redlink=1 "BlueCruise (page does not exist)") technology. Because of this assisted driving technology, Ford drivers can now legally take their hands off the wheel on certain roads. Its top speed is 129 km/h (80 mph). BlueCruise uses sensors and cameras to regulate the car's speed and to keep track of speed limits and road signs. It also monitors and keeps a safe distance from other vehicles. It also comes equipped with an eye-tracking system. If the driver stops looking at the road then the car will gradually reduce its speed. This technology will initially be offered in Ford's 2023 model of the electric Mustang Mach-E SUV. According to Thatcham Research, an automotive research company, this model is not a self-driving car. It is classified as a level 2 or partial automation assistance system. This means that technology controls two or more driving aspects but still requires human driver control in cases of emergencies. The driver is still legally responsible for accidents.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In August 2024 [Pennsylvania State Police](/wiki/Pennsylvania_State_Police "Pennsylvania State Police") filed charges against a driver that killed two men while using BlueCruise.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In November 2024, Ford announced that it would cut 800 jobs in the UK and 2,900 in Germany to try to reduce costs.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

In September 2025, Ford announced the relocation of its headquarters from the [Henry Ford II World Center](/wiki/Henry_Ford_II_World_Center "Henry Ford II World Center"), commonly known as the "Glass House," to a new facility in Dearborn.[[92]](#cite_note-freep-2025-09-15-92)[[93]](#cite_note-click-2025-09-15-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) The new [Ford World Headquarters](/wiki/Ford_World_Headquarters "Ford World Headquarters") opened on November 16, 2025,[[95]](#cite_note-:3-95) with all operations expected to relocate by mid-2026, after which the Glass House is slated to be demolished.[[92]](#cite_note-freep-2025-09-15-92)[[93]](#cite_note-click-2025-09-15-93)

In December 2025, Ford entered a JV disposition agreement with [SK On](/wiki/SK_On "SK On"), dividing the BlueOval SK plants and pivoting to [ESS](/wiki/Energy_storage "Energy storage").[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### Executive management

Members of the Ford board as of January 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit) are: [William Clay Ford Jr.](/wiki/William_Clay_Ford_Jr. "William Clay Ford Jr.") (executive chairman), [Jim Farley](/wiki/Jim_Farley_(businessman) "Jim Farley (businessman)") (president and CEO), [Kimberly Casiano](/wiki/Kimberly_Casiano "Kimberly Casiano"), [Adriana Cisneros](/wiki/Adriana_Cisneros "Adriana Cisneros"), Alexandra Ford English (daughter of William Clay Ford Jr.), Henry Ford III (son of [Edsel Ford II](/wiki/Edsel_Ford_II "Edsel Ford II")), William W. Helman IV, [Jon Huntsman Jr.](/wiki/Jon_Huntsman_Jr. "Jon Huntsman Jr."), [William E. Kennard](/wiki/William_E._Kennard "William E. Kennard"), John C. May, [Beth E. Mooney](/wiki/Beth_E._Mooney "Beth E. Mooney"), [John L. Thornton](/wiki/John_L._Thornton "John L. Thornton"), [John Veihmeyer](/wiki/John_Veihmeyer "John Veihmeyer"), Lynn Vojvodich Radakovich, and John S. Weinberg.[[102]](#cite_note-fmci-102)

[Jim Farley](/wiki/Jim_Farley_(businessman) "Jim Farley (businessman)") succeeded [Jim Hackett](/wiki/James_Hackett_(businessman) "James Hackett (businessman)") as the chief executive officer of the company in August 2020; he previously served as Ford's chief operating officer. Hackett stayed in the company as an advisor until the second quarter of 2021.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### Ownership

Ford is mainly owned by institutional investors, who own around 60% of shares. The largest shareholders in December 2023 were:[[104]](#cite_note-104)

* [The Vanguard Group](/wiki/The_Vanguard_Group "The Vanguard Group") (8.71%)
* [BlackRock](/wiki/BlackRock "BlackRock") (7.20%)
* [State Street Corporation](/wiki/State_Street_Corporation "State Street Corporation") (4.46%)
* Newport Trust (3.98%)
* [Charles Schwab Corporation](/wiki/Charles_Schwab_Corporation "Charles Schwab Corporation") (2.25%)
* [Geode Capital Management](/wiki/Geode_Capital_Management "Geode Capital Management") (1.94%)
* [Fisher Investments](/wiki/Fisher_Investments "Fisher Investments") (1.52%)
* [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley") (1.31%)
* [Norges Bank](/wiki/Norges_Bank "Norges Bank") (1.00%)
* [Northern Trust](/wiki/Northern_Trust "Northern Trust") (0.94%)

### Ford Philanthropy

The Ford Philanthropy, formerly known as the Ford Motor Company Fund (also known as Ford Fund, not affiliated with the [Ford Foundation](/wiki/Ford_Foundation "Ford Foundation")), based in Dearborn, Michigan, is the philanthropic arm of the Ford Motor Company.[[105]](#cite_note-fordfund2016-105) Established in 1949[[106]](#cite_note-106) by [Henry Ford II](/wiki/Henry_Ford_II "Henry Ford II"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the organization is a nonprofit corporate foundation[[107]](#cite_note-107) financed by contributions from Ford Motor Company. In 2017, it contributed $63 million[[108]](#cite_note-108) to various causes[[105]](#cite_note-fordfund2016-105) with a focus on education, driving safely and community building.

The Ford Driving Skills for Life program is a driver safety program aimed at teens that were developed together with the Governors Highway Safety Association and safety experts.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The Ford Volunteer Corps allows Ford employees and retirees to sign up for volunteering work on local projects in more than 40 countries.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The organization invests $18 million annually in education in the United States and around the world, but accepts applications only from nonprofit organizations registered in the U.S.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Education programs and scholarships include Alan Mulally Engineering Scholarship, Ford Blue Oval Scholars Program, Ford College Community Challenge (Ford C3), Ford Driving Dreams Tour, Ford Fund/Detroit Free Press Journalism Scholarship, Ford Next Generation Learning (Ford NGL), Grants to Individuals Program, HBCU Community Challenge, Smithsonian Latino Center Young Ambassadors Program, and William Clay Ford Automotive Design Scholarship.

On April 29, 2024, the Ford Fund announced its official name has changed to Ford Philanthropy.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### FordWorks Program

Ford Motor Company created the FordWorks program in 2016[[113]](#cite_note-FordWorks_RecruitingNews-113)[[114]](#cite_note-FordWorks_ClickOnDetroit-114) with the aim to bring people with disabilities back into the workforce.[[115]](#cite_note-FordWorks_FordCorperate-115) It was the first automotive program to focus on bringing people with autism in the workforce in the US. They targeted people with autism[[113]](#cite_note-FordWorks_RecruitingNews-113)[[114]](#cite_note-FordWorks_ClickOnDetroit-114) but have expanded their criteria to people with other disabilities.[[115]](#cite_note-FordWorks_FordCorperate-115) Ford has partnered with Upbound to broaden their hiring under the FordWorks program.[[115]](#cite_note-FordWorks_FordCorperate-115)
