Ford has had manufacturing [operations](/wiki/List_of_Ford_factories "List of Ford factories") worldwide, including in the United States, Canada, Mexico, China, India, the United Kingdom, Germany, Turkey, Brazil, Argentina, Australia, and South Africa. Ford also helped the Soviet Union to construct Russian automaker [GAZ](/wiki/GAZ "GAZ").

### North America

In May 2010, Ford reported that its sales increased 23% for the month, and that 37% of its sales came from fleet sales.[[117]](#cite_note-117) In June 2010, sales to individual customers at dealerships increased 13% while fleet sales rose by 32%.[[118]](#cite_note-118) In the first seven months of 2010, fleet sales of Ford for the same period rose 35% to 386,000 units while retail sales increase 19%.[[119]](#cite_note-AutomotiveNews-119) Fleet sales account for 39 percent of Chrysler's sales and 31 percent for GM's.[[119]](#cite_note-AutomotiveNews-119)

In 2025, Ford reported higher automobile sales due to stronger demand for its hybrid models and affordable pickup truck. Sales rose by 6% year-on-year.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

### Europe

Main article: [Ford of Europe](/wiki/Ford_of_Europe "Ford of Europe")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Aachen_Ford-Entwicklungszentrum.jpg/250px-Aachen_Ford-Entwicklungszentrum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aachen_Ford-Entwicklungszentrum.jpg)

The Ford Research Center in [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), Germany, photographed in 2006

At first, [Ford in Germany](/wiki/Ford_Germany "Ford Germany") and [Ford in Britain](/wiki/Ford_of_Britain "Ford of Britain") built different models from one another until 1965, when the [Ford Transit](/wiki/Ford_Transit "Ford Transit") and later the [Ford Escort](/wiki/Ford_Escort_(Europe) "Ford Escort (Europe)") and the [Ford Capri](/wiki/Ford_Capri "Ford Capri") became common to both companies. In 1970, the [Ford Taunus](/wiki/Ford_Taunus "Ford Taunus") and the [Ford Cortina](/wiki/Ford_Cortina "Ford Cortina") came into production with a common base construction, both models being produced in [left hand drive](/wiki/Left_hand_drive "Left hand drive") and [right hand drive](/wiki/Right_hand_drive "Right hand drive"). Later on, the models became identical and the respective models right- and left-hand-drive exclusively. Rationalisation of model ranges meant that production of many models in the UK switched to elsewhere in Europe, including Belgium and Spain as well as Germany. The [Ford Sierra](/wiki/Ford_Sierra "Ford Sierra") replaced the Taunus and Cortina in 1982, drawing criticism for its radical aerodynamic styling, which was soon given nicknames, the "Jellymould" and "The Salesman's Spaceship".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Ford%27s_Dunton_Technical_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_218069.jpg/250px-Ford%27s_Dunton_Technical_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_218069.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford%27s_Dunton_Technical_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_218069.jpg)

Ford's [Dunton Technical Centre](/wiki/Dunton_Technical_Centre "Dunton Technical Centre") in [Laindon](/wiki/Laindon "Laindon"), United Kingdom, the largest automotive research and development facility in the country,[[121]](#cite_note-121) pictured in 2006

In February 2002, Ford ended car production in the UK. It was the first time in 90 years that Ford cars had not been made in Britain, although production of the [Transit](/wiki/Ford_Transit "Ford Transit") van continued at the company's [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton") facility until mid-2013, engines at [Bridgend](/wiki/Bridgend "Bridgend") and [Dagenham](/wiki/Ford_Dagenham "Ford Dagenham"), and transmissions at [Halewood](/wiki/Halewood "Halewood"). Development of European Ford is broadly split between [Dunton](/wiki/Dunton_Wayletts "Dunton Wayletts") in Essex (powertrain, Fiesta/Ka, and commercial vehicles) and [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") (body, chassis, electrical, Focus, Mondeo) in Germany. Ford also produced the [Thames](/wiki/Thames_Trader "Thames Trader") range of commercial vehicles, although the use of this brand name was discontinued with the introduction of the [Ford Transit](/wiki/Ford_Transit "Ford Transit") in 1965. Elsewhere in continental Europe, Ford assembles the Mondeo, [Galaxy](/wiki/Ford_Galaxy "Ford Galaxy"), [S-Max](/wiki/Ford_S-Max "Ford S-Max")[[122]](#cite_note-122) and [Kuga](/wiki/Ford_Kuga "Ford Kuga")[[123]](#cite_note-123) in Valencia (Spain), Fiesta in Cologne (Germany), Focus in [Saarlouis](/wiki/Saarlouis "Saarlouis") (Germany), Ecosport[[124]](#cite_note-124) and [Puma](/wiki/Ford_Puma_(crossover) "Ford Puma (crossover)")[[125]](#cite_note-125) in [Craiova](/wiki/Craiova "Craiova") (Romania).

Ford also owns a joint-venture production plant in Turkey. [Ford Otosan](/wiki/Otosan "Otosan"), established in the 1970s, manufactures the [Transit Connect](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Connect "Ford Transit Connect") compact panel van as well as the "Jumbo" and long-wheelbase versions of the full-size Transit. This new production facility was set up near [Kocaeli](/wiki/%C4%B0zmit "İzmit") in 2002, and its opening marked the end of Transit assembly in Genk.

Another joint venture plant near [Setúbal](/wiki/Set%C3%BAbal "Setúbal") in Portugal, set up in collaboration with [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen"), formerly assembled the [Galaxy](/wiki/Ford_Galaxy "Ford Galaxy") people-carrier as well as its sister ships, the [VW Sharan](/wiki/Volkswagen_Sharan "Volkswagen Sharan") and [SEAT Alhambra](/wiki/SEAT_Alhambra "SEAT Alhambra"). With the introduction of the third generation of the Galaxy, Ford has moved the production of the people-carrier to the Genk plant, with Volkswagen taking over sole ownership of the Setúbal facility.

In 2008, Ford acquired a majority stake in [Automobile Craiova](/wiki/Automobile_Craiova "Automobile Craiova"), Romania. Starting 2009, the [Ford Transit Connect](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Connect "Ford Transit Connect") was Ford's first model produced in Craiova, followed, in 2012, by low-capacity car engines and a new small class car, the [B-Max](/wiki/Ford_B-Max "Ford B-Max").[[126]](#cite_note-126) In 2022, [Ford Romania](/wiki/Ford_Romania "Ford Romania") was acquired by Ford Otosan and in 2023, production of the [Ford Transit Courier/Tourneo Courier](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Courier#Second_generation_(2023) "Ford Transit Courier") started at the facility.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Its 1959 [Anglia](/wiki/Ford_Anglia "Ford Anglia") two-door saloon was one of the most quirky-looking small family cars in Europe at the time of its launch, but buyers soon became accustomed to its looks and it was hugely popular with British buyers in particular. It was still selling well when replaced by the more practical [Escort](/wiki/Ford_Escort_(Europe) "Ford Escort (Europe)") in 1967.

The third incarnation of the Ford Escort was launched in 1980 and marked the company's move from [rear-wheel-drive](/wiki/Rear-wheel-drive "Rear-wheel-drive") saloons to [front-wheel-drive](/wiki/Front-wheel-drive "Front-wheel-drive") hatchbacks in the small family car sector.

The fourth-generation Escort was produced from 1990 until 2000, although its successor—the [Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus_(international) "Ford Focus (international)")—had been on sale since 1998. On its launch, the Focus was arguably the most dramatic-looking and fine-handling small family cars on sale and sold in huge volumes right up to the launch of the next-generation Focus at the end of 2004.

The 1982 [Ford Sierra](/wiki/Ford_Sierra "Ford Sierra")—replacement for the long-running and massively popular [Cortina](/wiki/Ford_Cortina "Ford Cortina") and [Taunus](/wiki/Ford_Taunus "Ford Taunus") models—was a style-setter at the time of its launch. Its ultramodern aerodynamic design was a world away from a boxy, sharp-edged Cortina, and it was massively popular just about everywhere it was sold. A series of updates kept it looking relatively fresh until it was replaced by the front-wheel-drive [Mondeo](/wiki/Ford_Mondeo "Ford Mondeo") at the start of 1993.

The rise in popularity of small cars during the 1970s saw Ford enter the mini-car market in 1976 with its [Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta") hatchback. Most of its production was concentrated at [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia,_Spain "Valencia, Spain") in Spain, and the Fiesta sold in huge figures from the very start. An update in 1983 and the launch of an all-new model in 1989 strengthened its position in the small car market.

On October 24, 2012, Ford announced that it would close its [Genk](/wiki/Genk "Genk") assembly plant in eastern Belgium by the end of 2014.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

In 2015, Ford announced that it took control of [Ford Sollers](/wiki/Ford_Sollers "Ford Sollers"), Ford's joint venture with Russian company [Sollers](/wiki/Sollers_JSC "Sollers JSC").[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)

In September 2018, at the IAA Commercial Vehicles show in Hannover, Germany, Ford introduced an electric tractor-trailer concept vehicle dubbed the F-Vision, which would have Level 4 autonomous driving capability.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

On March 1, 2022, Ford announced that it was suspending its [Sollers](/wiki/Ford_Sollers "Ford Sollers") joint venture operation in Russia, in response to the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133) On October 26, 2022, Ford sold its 49 percent share in the joint venture, and exited the Russian market,[[134]](#cite_note-oct2022-134) adding that it retains the option to buy them back within a 5-year period "should the global situation change".[[135]](#cite_note-135)

### East and Southeast Asia

Ford formed its first passenger-vehicle joint venture in China in 2001, six years behind GM and more than a decade after VW. It has spent as of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit) $4.9 billion to expand its lineup and double production capacity in China to 600,000 vehicles. This includes Ford's largest-ever factory complex in the southwestern city of Chongqing. Ford had 2.5% of the Chinese market in 2013, while VW controlled 14.5% and GM had 15.6%, according to consultant LMC Automotive. GM outsells Ford in China by more than six-to-one.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Ford's presence in Asia has traditionally been much smaller, confined to Malaysia, Singapore, Hong Kong, the Philippines, and Taiwan, where Ford has had a joint venture with [Lio Ho](/wiki/Ford_Lio_Ho_Motor "Ford Lio Ho Motor") since the 1970s. Ford began assembly of cars in Thailand in 1960, but withdrew from the country in 1976, and did not return until 1995 when it formed a joint venture with [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda") called Auto Alliance.[[137]](#cite_note-137) Now based in the Bo-win Sub District of the Sriracha District in [Chonburi](/wiki/Chonburi_(city) "Chonburi (city)"), the factory still produces passenger automobiles. The factory, built in 1941 in Singapore, was soon taken over by the Japanese during the war and was the site of a surrender of the British to the Japanese, at the [factory site](/wiki/Old_Ford_Motor_Factory "Old Ford Motor Factory") which is now a national monument in Singapore.
On April 30, 2013, Ford Motor Co. launched their car and pickup truck line in [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"). Previously, heavy importation taxes had stifled imported car purchases in Myanmar, but due to currency reform, lifting of previous import restrictions, and the abolishment of [shadow currency](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_controls "Foreign exchange controls"), Myanmar's car market had grown in demand.[[138]](#cite_note-investvine-138)

Ford decided to shut down their entire operations in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), including their dealer network by second half of 2016. Since 2016, Ford operation in Indonesia has been taken over by RMA Group. RMA Group would resume the distribution new Ford cars in 2020, 4 years since last Ford left Indonesian market[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140)

#### South Korea

In 1967, Ford partnered with the South Korean company [Hyundai](/wiki/Hyundai_Motor_Company "Hyundai Motor Company"), and at the new factory in [Ulsan, South Korea](/wiki/Ulsan,_South_Korea "Ulsan, South Korea"), built the European [Ford Cortina](/wiki/Ford_Cortina "Ford Cortina") until 1974 when Hyundai introduced their all-new [Hyundai Pony](/wiki/Hyundai_Pony "Hyundai Pony") in 1975. Ford then developed a relationship with Korea's oldest car manufacturer [Kia](/wiki/Kia_Motors "Kia Motors") which built vehicles co-engineered with [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda"), later selling the [Ford Festiva](/wiki/Ford_Festiva "Ford Festiva") from 1988 to 1993, and the [Ford Aspire](/wiki/Ford_Festiva "Ford Festiva") from 1994 to 1997 for export to the United States. With the acquisition of a stake in Japanese manufacturer [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda") in 1979, Ford began selling Mazda's [Familia](/wiki/Mazda_Familia "Mazda Familia") and [Capella](/wiki/Mazda_Capella "Mazda Capella") as the [Ford Laser](/wiki/Ford_Laser "Ford Laser") and [Telstar](/wiki/Ford_Telstar "Ford Telstar") throughout the region, replacing the European-sourced Escort and Cortina. From 1989 to 1996, Kia imported the [Mercury Sable](/wiki/Mercury_Sable "Mercury Sable") from Ford in the U.S. and sold them in South Korea as the Kia Sable. Though the Sable was branded and marketed as a Kia, it retained the Mercury badges and emblem. Ford lost their Kia interest to Hyundai in 1998 after the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"). Kia had declared bankruptcy in 1997; in 1998, [Hyundai Motor Company](/wiki/Hyundai_Motor_Company "Hyundai Motor Company") acquired 51% of the company, outbidding Ford which had owned an interest in Kia Motors since 1986.[[141]](#cite_note-141) After subsequent divestments,[[142]](#cite_note-142) Hyundai Motor Company owns less than 50% of the company but remains Kia's largest stakeholder.

As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), Ford sells the Explorer, Mondeo, and Mustang,[[143]](#cite_note-143) as well as the Lincoln Aviator, Continental, Corsair, MKZ, and Nautilus in South Korea.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

### South and West Asia

[Ford India](/wiki/Ford_India_Private_Limited "Ford India Private Limited") began production in 1998 at [Chennai](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"), with its Ford Escort model, which was later replaced by the locally produced [Ford Ikon](/wiki/Ford_Ikon "Ford Ikon") in 2001. It has since added the Fusion, Fiesta, Mondeo and Endeavour models to its product line.

On March 9, 2010, Ford launched its first made-for-India compact car. Starting at [₹](/wiki/Indian_rupee "Indian rupee")349,900, the [Figo](/wiki/Ford_Figo "Ford Figo") was Ford's first car designed and priced for the mass Indian market.[[145]](#cite_note-145) On July 28, 2011, Ford India signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with the State of [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") for the construction of an assembly and engine plant in [Sanand](/wiki/Sanand "Sanand") and planned to invest approximately US$1 billion on a 460-acre site.[[146]](#cite_note-146) In 2019, the company and [Mahindra & Mahindra](/wiki/Mahindra_%26_Mahindra "Mahindra & Mahindra") formed a joint venture to develop, market and distribute Ford-branded vehicles in India.[[147]](#cite_note-147) In September 2021 Ford India announced plans to shut down both its assembly plants. The company said that it intends to maintain its parts and service network.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

Ford's market presence in the Middle East has traditionally been small, partly due to previous Arab boycotts of companies dealing with Israel. Ford and Lincoln vehicles are currently marketed in ten countries in the region.[[149]](#cite_note-149) Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates are the biggest markets. Ford also established itself in Egypt in 1926 but faced an uphill battle during the 1950s due to the hostile nationalist business environment.[[150]](#cite_note-150) Ford's distributor in Saudi Arabia announced in February 2003 that it had sold 100,000 Ford and Lincoln vehicles since commencing sales in November 1986. Half of the Ford and Lincoln vehicles sold in that country were [Ford Crown Victorias](/wiki/Ford_Crown_Victoria "Ford Crown Victoria").[[151]](#cite_note-151) In 2004, Ford sold 30,000 units in the region, falling far short of General Motors' 88,852 units and [Nissan Motors](/wiki/Nissan "Nissan")' 75,000 units.

### South America

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Ford_planta_pacheco_1960s.jpg/250px-Ford_planta_pacheco_1960s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_planta_pacheco_1960s.jpg)

Ford assembly plant in General Pacheco, Argentina, as seen in the 1960s

In South America, Ford's primary operations are in [Brazil](/wiki/Ford_Brasil "Ford Brasil"), [Argentina](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Argentina "Ford Motor Argentina"), and Ford Andina[[152]](#cite_note-152) (Colombia, Ecuador, and Venezuela). Ford employs over 18,000 people and operates seven assembly or other plants in the region.[[153]](#cite_note-153) In 1987, Ford Brasil and Ford Motor Argentina merged their operations with the Brazilian and Argentine operations of [Volkswagen Group](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group "Volkswagen Group"), forming a new joint-venture company called [Autolatina](/wiki/Autolatina "Autolatina") with a shared model range.[[154]](#cite_note-FolhadeS.Paulo-154) Autolatina was dissolved in 1995.[[154]](#cite_note-FolhadeS.Paulo-154)

In early 2021, Ford's Brazil branch announced it would completely cease production in the country over the course of a few months.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

### Africa

In Africa, Ford's market presence has traditionally been strongest in South Africa and neighbouring countries, with only trucks being sold elsewhere on the continent. Ford in South Africa began by importing kits from Canada to be assembled at its facility in [Port Elizabeth](/wiki/Port_Elizabeth "Port Elizabeth"); the company later sourced its models from the UK and Australia, with local versions of the Ford Cortina including the XR6, with a 3.0 V6 engine, and a [Cortina-based 'bakkie' or pick-up](/wiki/Ford_P100 "Ford P100"), which was exported to the UK. In the mid-1980s Ford merged with a rival company, owned by [Anglo American](/wiki/Anglo_American_plc "Anglo American plc"), to form the [South African Motor Corporation](/wiki/South_African_Motor_Corporation "South African Motor Corporation") ([Samcor](/wiki/Samcor "Samcor")).[[156]](#cite_note-156)

Following international condemnation of [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid"), Ford divested from South Africa in 1988, and sold its stake in Samcor, although it licensed the use of its brand name to the company.[[157]](#cite_note-157) Samcor began to assemble Mazdas as well, which affected its product line-up and saw the European Fords like the Escort and Sierra replaced by the [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda")-based Laser[[158]](#cite_note-158) and [Telstar](/wiki/Ford_Telstar "Ford Telstar").[[159]](#cite_note-159) Ford bought a 45 per cent stake in [Samcor](/wiki/Samcor "Samcor") following the demise of apartheid in 1994, and this later became, once again, a wholly owned subsidiary, the Ford Motor Company of Southern Africa. Ford now sells a local sedan version of the Fiesta (also built in India and Mexico), and the Focus. The Falcon model from Australia was also sold in South Africa, but was dropped in 2003; the Mondeo, after briefly being assembled locally, was dropped in 2005. The Mondeo was later reintroduced in 2015, badged as the [Fusion](/wiki/Ford_Fusion_(Americas) "Ford Fusion (Americas)"), but was dropped in 2017.[[160]](#cite_note-160)

### Research

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Dubai_Future_Forum_2024_-_Jennifer_Brace.jpg/250px-Dubai_Future_Forum_2024_-_Jennifer_Brace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dubai_Future_Forum_2024_-_Jennifer_Brace.jpg)

Jennifer Brace, chief futurist at Ford, [Dubai Future Forum](/wiki/Dubai_Future_Forum "Dubai Future Forum") (2024)

Ford Research and Innovation Center is the name of the technology research facilities of Ford Motor Company in

* [Dearborn, Michigan](/wiki/Dearborn,_Michigan "Dearborn, Michigan")
* [Palo Alto, California](/wiki/Palo_Alto,_California "Palo Alto, California")
* [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), Germany
* [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), China

The Ford Research and Innovation Center in Palo Alto was first opened in 2012, and in January 2015, announced plans to significantly expand its operations.[[161]](#cite_note-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162)[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164)

Ford has the position of chief futurist in charge of forecasting which was started in 2019 by Sheryl Connelly, and is currently held by Jennifer Brace.[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166)

## Former operations

### East and Southeast Asia

#### Ford of Japan

Main article: [Ford Motor Company of Japan](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company_of_Japan "Ford Motor Company of Japan")

Ford established a manufacturing facility in the port city of Yokohama in February 1925, where Model T vehicles were assembled using imported [knock-down kits](/wiki/Knock-down_kit "Knock-down kit").[[167]](#cite_note-167) The factory subsequently produced 10,000 Model A's up to 1936. Production ceased in 1940 as a result of political tensions between Japan and the United States.

After World War II, Ford did not have a presence in Japan, as the Ford facility was appropriated by the Japanese Government until 1958 when property was returned as a possession of the Ford Motor Company and became a [research and development location](/wiki/List_of_Mazda_facilities#R&D "List of Mazda facilities") for Ford partner [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda"). In 1979, Ford acquired a 24.5% ownership stake in Mazda, and in 1982, Ford and Mazda jointly established a sales channel to sell Ford products in Japan, including vehicles manufactured in North America, at a dealership called *Autorama ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): [オートラマ](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E3%82%AA%E3%83%BC%E3%83%88%E3%83%A9%E3%83%9E "ja:オートラマ"))*. The Autorama sales channel was renamed Ford Sales of Japan in 1997.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

Vehicles sold at *Autorama* locations were the North American assembled Ford Explorer, Probe (1989–1998), Mustang, Taurus (1989–1997), Thunderbird (1990–1993), Lincoln Continental, and Lincoln LS. Ford products manufactured in Europe that were sold in Japan were the [Ford Mondeo](/wiki/Ford_Mondeo "Ford Mondeo"), [Ka](/wiki/Ford_Ka "Ford Ka"), [Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus "Ford Focus"), [Focus C-MAX](/wiki/Ford_C-Max "Ford C-Max"), [Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta"), and the [Galaxy](/wiki/Ford_Galaxy "Ford Galaxy"). Mazda manufactured Ford vehicles in Japan and sold them as Fords at the Autorama locations. They were the [Ford Telstar](/wiki/Ford_Telstar "Ford Telstar") (Mazda Capella), [Laser](/wiki/Ford_Laser "Ford Laser"), [Festiva](/wiki/Ford_Festiva "Ford Festiva"), [Festiva Mini Wagon](/wiki/Mazda_Demio "Mazda Demio"), [Ixion](/wiki/Ford_Ixion "Ford Ixion") (Mazda Premacy), [Freda](/wiki/Ford_Freda "Ford Freda") (Mazda Bongo Friendee), [Spectron](/wiki/Ford_Spectron "Ford Spectron") (Mazda Bongo), and commercial trucks J80 and the J100 (Mazda Bongo truck).

Ford increased its shareholding in Mazda to 33.4% in 1996, but as of July 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), it is listed at 11%.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Ford did sell a small range of vehicles in Japan; as of October 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), the Ford Mustang, Escape, Explorer (and Explorer pickup truck), [Ford Kuga](/wiki/Ford_Kuga "Ford Kuga"), [Lincoln Navigator](/wiki/Lincoln_Navigator "Lincoln Navigator"), [Lincoln MKX](/wiki/Lincoln_MKX "Lincoln MKX"), and more recently, the [Ford Ecosport](/wiki/Ford_Ecosport "Ford Ecosport") were available in Japan. As of February 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), Ford no longer maintains a regional office in Minato, Tokyo, Japan, and sales of new cars in Japan have ended.[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171)

### Oceania

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Ford_dealer_1936_SLNSW_FL1373714.jpg/250px-Ford_dealer_1936_SLNSW_FL1373714.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_dealer_1936_SLNSW_FL1373714.jpg)

Frank Delandro Authorised Ford dealer, Crows Nest, Sydney, 1936

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/2014_Ford_Falcon_%28FG_X%29_XR6_Turbo_sedan_%2823382738252%29.jpg/250px-2014_Ford_Falcon_%28FG_X%29_XR6_Turbo_sedan_%2823382738252%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2014_Ford_Falcon_(FG_X)_XR6_Turbo_sedan_(23382738252).jpg)

Ford FG X Falcon (Australia) (2014–2016; 2014 model shown)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Ford_stamping_plant_Geelong.jpg/250px-Ford_stamping_plant_Geelong.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_stamping_plant_Geelong.jpg)

The Ford stamping plant in [Geelong](/wiki/Geelong "Geelong"), Victoria, Australia. It closed in 2016.

In Australia and New Zealand, the popular [Ford Falcon](/wiki/Ford_Falcon_(Australia) "Ford Falcon (Australia)") (1960–2016) had long been considered the average family car and is considerably larger than the Mondeo, Ford's largest car sold in Europe. Between 1960 and 1972, the Falcon was based on the U.S. model of the [same name](/wiki/Ford_Falcon_(North_America) "Ford Falcon (North America)"), but since then was entirely designed and manufactured in Australia until 2016, occasionally being manufactured in New Zealand. Like its [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") rival, the [Holden Commodore](/wiki/Holden_Commodore "Holden Commodore"), the Falcon used a rear-wheel-drive layout. High-performance variants of the Falcon running locally built engines (the [Barra engine](/wiki/Ford_Barra_engine "Ford Barra engine")) produced up to 436 hp (325 kW). A [ute](/wiki/Ute_(vehicle) "Ute (vehicle)") ([coupé utility](/wiki/Coup%C3%A9_utility "Coupé utility")) version was also available with the same range of drivetrains. In addition, Ford Australia sold highly tuned limited-production Falcon sedans and utes through its performance car division, [Ford Performance Vehicles](/wiki/Ford_Performance_Vehicles "Ford Performance Vehicles") from 2002 until it closed in 2014.

In Australia, the Commodore and Falcon had traditionally outsold all other cars and constituted over 20% of the new car market. In New Zealand, Ford was second in market share in the first eight months of 2006 with 14.4%.[[172]](#cite_note-172) More recently, Ford has axed its Falcon-based LWB variant of its lineup– the [Fairlane](/wiki/Ford_Fairlane_(Australia) "Ford Fairlane (Australia)") and LTD ranges. Ford discontinued the Fairlane in 2007 and LTD in 2008. Ford had announced that their Geelong engine manufacturing plant would be shut down between 2013 and 2016. They had earlier announced local manufacturing of the Focus small car starting from 2011, but instead decided to import the model from Ford's plant in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[173]](#cite_note-173)

In Australia, the Laser was one of [Ford Australia](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company_of_Australia "Ford Motor Company of Australia")'s most successful models and was manufactured in Ford's [Homebush West](/wiki/Homebush_West,_New_South_Wales "Homebush West, New South Wales") plant from 1981 until the plant's closure in September 1994. It outsold the [Mazda 323](/wiki/Mazda_323 "Mazda 323"), despite being almost identical to it because the Laser was manufactured in Australia and Ford was perceived as a local brand.[[174]](#cite_note-174) According to research carried out by [Ford Australia](/wiki/Ford_Australia "Ford Australia") in 1984, a third of Laser buyers were unaware that the Ford model was based on the [Mazda 323](/wiki/Mazda_323 "Mazda 323").[[175]](#cite_note-175)

In New Zealand, the [Ford Laser](/wiki/Ford_Laser "Ford Laser") and [Telstar](/wiki/Ford_Telstar "Ford Telstar") were assembled alongside the [Mazda 323](/wiki/Mazda_323 "Mazda 323") and [626](/wiki/Mazda_626 "Mazda 626") until 1997, at the Vehicle Assemblers of New Zealand (VANZ) plant in [Wiri](/wiki/Wiri "Wiri"), [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland").[[176]](#cite_note-176) The [Sierra](/wiki/Ford_Sierra "Ford Sierra") wagon was also assembled in New Zealand, owing to the popularity of [station wagons](/wiki/Station_wagon "Station wagon") in that market.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The scheduled closure of Ford's Australian manufacturing base in 2016 was confirmed on May 23, 2013. Headquartered in the Victorian suburb of Broadmeadows, the company had registered losses worth AU$600 million over the five years prior to the announcement. It was noted that the corporate fleet and government sales that account for two-thirds of large, local car sales in Australia are insufficient to keep Ford's products profitable and viable in Australia. The decision affected 1200 Ford workers—over 600 employees in Geelong and more than 500 in Broadmeadows—who lost their jobs by October 2016. The closure of Ford's plants in Norlane Geelong and Broadmeadows Melbourne occurred on October 7, 2016.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

## Products and services

### Automobiles

See also: [List of Ford vehicles](/wiki/List_of_Ford_vehicles "List of Ford vehicles"), [List of Lincoln vehicles](/wiki/List_of_Lincoln_vehicles "List of Lincoln vehicles"), and [List of Mercury vehicles](/wiki/List_of_Mercury_vehicles "List of Mercury vehicles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Prag%2C_Wenzelsplatz%2C_Ford_Mustang_--_2019_--_101913.jpg/250px-Prag%2C_Wenzelsplatz%2C_Ford_Mustang_--_2019_--_101913.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prag,_Wenzelsplatz,_Ford_Mustang_--_2019_--_101913.jpg)

[Ford Mustangs](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") of various years on display at Wenceslas Square, Prague, Czech Republic in 2019. The vehicle is a popular model sold in many countries.

Ford Motor Company sells a broad range of automobiles under the Ford [marque](/wiki/Marque "Marque") worldwide, and an additional range of luxury automobiles under the [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_(automobile) "Lincoln (automobile)") marque in the United States. The company has sold vehicles under a number of other marques during its history. The [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(automobile) "Mercury (automobile)") brand was introduced by Ford in 1939, continuing in production until 2011 when poor sales led to its discontinuation.[[179]](#cite_note-179) In 1958, Ford introduced the [Edsel](/wiki/Edsel "Edsel") brand, but poor sales led to its discontinuation in 1960. In 1985, the [Merkur](/wiki/Merkur "Merkur") brand was introduced in the United States to market products produced by Ford of Europe; it was discontinued in 1989.

Ford acquired the British sports car maker [Aston Martin](/wiki/Aston_Martin "Aston Martin") in 1989, later selling it on March 12, 2007,[[180]](#cite_note-180) although retaining an 8% stake.[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182) Ford purchased [Volvo Cars](/wiki/Volvo_Cars "Volvo Cars") of Sweden in 1999,[[183]](#cite_note-183) selling it to [Zhejiang Geely Holding Group](/wiki/Zhejiang_Geely_Holding_Group "Zhejiang Geely Holding Group") in 2010.

In November 2008, it reduced its 33.4% [controlling interest](/wiki/Controlling_interest "Controlling interest") in [Mazda](/wiki/Mazda "Mazda") of Japan to a 13.4% non-controlling interest.[[184]](#cite_note-184) On November 18, 2010, Ford reduced their stake further to just 3%, citing the reduction of ownership would allow greater flexibility to pursue growth in emerging markets. Ford and Mazda remain strategic partners through exchanges of technological information and joint ventures, including an American joint venture plant in [Flat Rock, Michigan](/wiki/Flat_Rock,_Michigan "Flat Rock, Michigan") called [Auto Alliance](/wiki/Auto_Alliance "Auto Alliance").[[185]](#cite_note-185) In 2015, Ford sold its remaining 3% stake in Mazda.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

Ford sold the United Kingdom-based [Jaguar](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") and [Land Rover](/wiki/Land_Rover "Land Rover") companies and brands to [Tata Motors](/wiki/Tata_Motors "Tata Motors") of India in March 2008.

On April 25, 2018, Ford announced that it planned to phase out all but one of its North American automobile models (the [Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") will be the sole surviving model) to focus primarily on pickup trucks and SUVs. Ford had also planned to introduce an "Active" crossover version of the next-generation [Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus "Ford Focus"), but canceled those plans due to tariff issues between the United States and China.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

### Trucks

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Ford_truck_1961_ad.jpg/250px-Ford_truck_1961_ad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_truck_1961_ad.jpg)

An advertisement for the 1961 Ford H-Series truck

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/2023_Ford_F-150_Lightning.jpg/250px-2023_Ford_F-150_Lightning.jpg)](/wiki/File:2023_Ford_F-150_Lightning.jpg)

2023 [Ford F-150 Lightning](/wiki/Ford_F-150_Lightning "Ford F-150 Lightning")

Ford has produced trucks since 1908, beginning with the [Ford Model TT](/wiki/Ford_Model_TT "Ford Model TT"), followed by the [Model AA](/wiki/Ford_Model_AA "Ford Model AA"), and the [Model BB](/wiki/1932_Ford#Model_BB_trucks "1932 Ford"). Countries where Ford commercial vehicles are or were formerly produced include Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Canada (also badged as [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(automobile) "Mercury (automobile)")), France, Germany, India, Netherlands, Philippines, Spain (badged [Ebro](/wiki/Ebro_trucks "Ebro trucks") too), Turkey, UK (badged also [Fordson](/wiki/Fordson "Fordson") and [Thames](/wiki/Thames_Trader "Thames Trader")), and the United States.

From the 1940s to the late 1970s, Ford's [Ford F-Series](/wiki/Ford_F-Series "Ford F-Series") was used as the base for light trucks for the North American market.

Most of these ventures are now extinct. The European one that lasted longest was the lorries arm of [Ford of Britain](/wiki/Ford_of_Britain "Ford of Britain"), which became part of the [Iveco](/wiki/Iveco "Iveco") group in 1986. Ford had a minority share in the new company and Iveco took over sales and production of the [Ford Cargo](/wiki/Ford_Cargo "Ford Cargo") range.[[188]](#cite_note-188) Ford's last significant European truck models were the [Transcontinental](/wiki/Ford_Transcontinental "Ford Transcontinental") and the Cargo. At the end of 1996, Ford sold the rights to its heavy trucks division to the [Freightliner Trucks](/wiki/Freightliner_Trucks "Freightliner Trucks") division of Daimler AG, with Ford producing the Cargo, Louisville, and Aeromax, through the 1998 model year. During the 1998 model year, Freightliner began production of its own versions of Ford-developed trucks in St. Thomas, Ontario, launching the [Sterling](/wiki/Sterling_Trucks "Sterling Trucks") truck brand.[[189]](#cite_note-sterling_news_release-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190) Slotted between Freightliner and Western Star, Sterling trucks were produced through 2009.

Line of heavy trucks made by Ford for the North American market:

* Ford F-Series
  + "Big Job" (1951–1957)
  + "Super Duty/Extra Heavy Duty (1958–1962)
* Ford N-Series (1963–1969)
* [Ford L-Series trucks](/wiki/Ford_L-Series "Ford L-Series") (1970–1998)
  + aka Ford "Louisville Line"
  + Ford Aeromax (1988–1998)
  + Ford Louisville (1996–1998)
  + Sterling (1998–2009)
* [Ford C-Series](/wiki/Ford_C-Series "Ford C-Series") (1957–1990)
* [Ford Cargo/CF-Series](/wiki/Ford_Cargo "Ford Cargo") (1986–1997)
* Ford H-Series (1961–1966)
  + aka "Two-story Falcon"
* Ford W-Series (1966–1977)
* Ford CL-Series (1978–1995)

For 1999, Ford briefly withdrew from production of medium-duty trucks. For the 2001 model year, the company entered into a joint venture with [Navistar International](/wiki/Navistar_International "Navistar International") (the supplier of diesel engines for 1-ton F-Series trucks), named *Blue Diamond Truck Company LLC*.[[191]](#cite_note-BD1-191) As part of the joint venture, sharing a common truck chassis, the two companies would produce medium-duty (Class 6–7) trucks in a Navistar facility in Mexico, with each manufacturer supplying its own powertrain and bodywork, with the [Ford F-650/F-750 Super Duty](/wiki/Ford_F-650 "Ford F-650") and [International 4000/DuraStar](/wiki/International_DuraStar "International DuraStar") sharing an assembly line. In 2006, the joint venture debuted the Ford LCF/International CityStar.[[192]](#cite_note-LCF1-192) Using a modified F-Series chassis adopted to fit a Mazda Titan cab, the LCF was a low-cab forward truck that was sold through 2009.[[193]](#cite_note-LCF2-193)

In Europe, Ford manufactures the [Ford Transit](/wiki/Ford_Transit "Ford Transit") jumbo van, which is classed as a Large Goods Vehicle and has a payload of up to 2,265 kg; there are options of a panel van, pickup or chassis cab. The Ford Transit is also available as a light van called the [Ford Transit Connect](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Connect "Ford Transit Connect") and the [Ford Ranger](/wiki/Ford_Ranger "Ford Ranger") pickup is available.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

### Buses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/1980sThomasFordSheffield.jpg/250px-1980sThomasFordSheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:1980sThomasFordSheffield.jpg)

A Ford B700 bus chassis, with a body by [Thomas Built](/wiki/Thomas_Built_Buses "Thomas Built Buses")

Ford manufactured complete buses in the company's early history, but today the role of the company has changed to that of a [second stage manufacturer](/wiki/Second_stage_manufacturer "Second stage manufacturer"). In North America, the [E-Series](/wiki/Ford_E-Series "Ford E-Series") is still used as a chassis for small school buses and the [F-650](/wiki/Ford_F-650 "Ford F-650") is used in commercial bus markets. In the 1980s and 1990s, the medium-duty [B700](/wiki/Ford_B-Series "Ford B-Series") was a popular chassis used by school bus body manufacturers including [Thomas Built](/wiki/Thomas_Built_Buses "Thomas Built Buses"), [Ward](/wiki/Ward_Body_Works "Ward Body Works"), and [Blue Bird](/wiki/Blue_Bird_Corporation "Blue Bird Corporation"), but Ford lost its market share due to industry contraction and agreements between body manufacturers. Older bus models included:

Prior to 1936, Ford buses were based on truck bodies:

* Model B – 1930s
* Model T – 1920s
* F-105 school bus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/1937_Ford_Transit_Bus_in_Seattle%2C_when_new.jpg/250px-1937_Ford_Transit_Bus_in_Seattle%2C_when_new.jpg)](/wiki/File:1937_Ford_Transit_Bus_in_Seattle,_when_new.jpg)

A 1937 [Ford Transit Bus](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Bus "Ford Transit Bus") in Seattle

In 1936, Ford introduced the [Ford Transit Bus](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Bus "Ford Transit Bus"), a series of small transit buses with bodies built by a second party. Originally a front-engine design, it was modified to a rear-engine design in 1939. About 1,000 to 1,200 of the original design were built, and around 12,500 of the rear-engine design, which was in production until 1947[[195]](#cite_note-MCA-195) (rebranded as the Universal Bus in 1946).

Rear-engine Transit Bus chassis model numbers:[[196]](#cite_note-detroittransithistory.info-196)

* 09-B/19-B City transit bus – 1939–1941
* 19-B/29-B City transit bus – 1941–1942
* 49-B/79-B City transit bus – 1944–1947
* 69-B City transit bus – 1946–1947
* 29-B City transit bus – 1946–1947
* 72-T transit bus – 1944–1945

After 1946 the Transit City bus was sold as the *Universal Bus* with the roof changed from fabric/wood to all-metal:

* 79-B Universal transit bus – 1946–1947

Succeeding the [Ford Transit Bus](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Bus "Ford Transit Bus") was the [Ford 8M](/w/index.php?title=Ford_8M&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ford 8M (page does not exist)") buses:

* 8MB transit bus – with Wayne Works 1948–?

Following World War II and from the 1950s onwards, Ford lost out to [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors").[[196]](#cite_note-detroittransithistory.info-196) This led to the end of transit buses for Ford in North America.

* [B500 or B-series](/wiki/Ford_B-Series "Ford B-Series") – 1950–1990s based on Ford F-series truck chassis used by school bus body manufacturers

In Europe, Ford manufactures the [Ford Transit](/wiki/Ford_Transit "Ford Transit") [Minibus](/wiki/Minibus "Minibus") which is classed in Europe as a Passenger Carrying Vehicle and there are options of 12-, 15-, or 17-seaters.[[197]](#cite_note-197) In the past, European models included:

* EM
* N-138
* D series buses (Australia)

### Tractors

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Ford-Tractor.jpg/250px-Ford-Tractor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford-Tractor.jpg)

A Ford N series tractor

The "Henry Ford and Son Company" began making [Fordson tractors](/wiki/Fordson_tractor "Fordson tractor") in Henry's hometown of Springwells (later part of [Dearborn](/wiki/Dearborn,_Michigan "Dearborn, Michigan")), Michigan, from 1907 to 1928, from 1919 to 1932, at [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"), Ireland, and between 1933 and 1964 at [Dagenham](/wiki/Ford_Dagenham "Ford Dagenham"), England, later transferred to [Basildon](/wiki/Basildon "Basildon"). They were also produced in [Leningrad](/wiki/Leningrad "Leningrad") beginning in 1924.

Ford reentered the tractor market in 1939 with the [Ford N-series tractors](/wiki/Ford_N-series_tractor "Ford N-series tractor"). The Ford 8N, introduced in 1947, became the most popular tractor of all time in North America. Production of the N line of models ended in 1952.

The [Ford NAA tractor](/wiki/Ford_NAA_tractor "Ford NAA tractor") was introduced as an entirely new model in 1953. It was a replacement for the Ford N-Series tractors. Larger than the 8N, with a four-cylinder engine, and streamlined styling.

In 1986, Ford expanded its tractor business when it purchased the Sperry-New Holland skid-steer loader and hay baler, hay tools and implement company from [Sperry Corporation](/wiki/Sperry_Corporation "Sperry Corporation") and formed Ford-New Holland which bought out [Versatile](/wiki/Versatile_(company) "Versatile (company)") tractors in 1988. This company was bought by [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") in 1993 and the name changed from Ford New Holland to New Holland. [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_Ag "New Holland Ag") is now part of [CNH Global](/wiki/CNH_Global "CNH Global").

Ford offers automotive finance through [Ford Motor Credit Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Credit_Company "Ford Motor Credit Company").

## Motorsport

Main article: [Ford Racing](/wiki/Ford_Racing "Ford Racing")

### FIA World Championships

Along with [Shelby](/wiki/Carroll_Shelby_International "Carroll Shelby International")[[200]](#cite_note-200) and [Chevrolet](/wiki/Chevrolet "Chevrolet"),[[201]](#cite_note-201) Ford is one of only three American constructors to win titles on the international scene at the [FIA World Championships](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l%27Automobile#FIA_World_Championships "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile"). As a constructor, Ford won the [World Sportscar Championship](/wiki/World_Sportscar_Championship "World Sportscar Championship") three times: in [1966](/wiki/1966_World_Sportscar_Championship_season "1966 World Sportscar Championship season") (both [sports prototype](/wiki/Sports_prototype "Sports prototype") category and [Grand Touring (GT)](/wiki/Grand_tourer "Grand tourer") category), [1967](/wiki/1967_World_Sportscar_Championship_season "1967 World Sportscar Championship season") (GT category), and [1968](/wiki/1968_World_Sportscar_Championship_season "1968 World Sportscar Championship season") (sports prototype category). At the [World Rally Championship](/wiki/World_Rally_Championship "World Rally Championship") Ford won 4 [World Drivers' Championships](/wiki/List_of_World_Rally_Championship_Drivers%27_champions "List of World Rally Championship Drivers' champions") (in [1979](/wiki/1979_World_Rally_Championship_season "1979 World Rally Championship season"), [1981](/wiki/1981_World_Rally_Championship_season "1981 World Rally Championship season"), [2017](/wiki/2017_World_Rally_Championship_season "2017 World Rally Championship season") and [2018](/wiki/2018_World_Rally_Championship_season "2018 World Rally Championship season")) as well as 4 [World Manufacturers' Championships](/wiki/List_of_World_Rally_Championship_Manufacturers%27_champions "List of World Rally Championship Manufacturers' champions") (in [1979](/wiki/1979_World_Rally_Championship_season "1979 World Rally Championship season"), [2006](/wiki/2006_World_Rally_Championship_season "2006 World Rally Championship season"), [2007](/wiki/2007_World_Rally_Championship_season "2007 World Rally Championship season") and [2017](/wiki/2017_World_Rally_Championship_season "2017 World Rally Championship season")). At the [World Touring Car Championship](/wiki/World_Touring_Car_Championship "World Touring Car Championship") Ford helped a Swiss team [Eggenberger Motorsport](/wiki/Eggenberger_Motorsport "Eggenberger Motorsport") win entrants' title in [1987](/wiki/1987_World_Touring_Car_Championship "1987 World Touring Car Championship"). As an engine supplier, Ford also won 13 [Formula One World Drivers' Championships](/wiki/List_of_Formula_One_World_Drivers%27_Champions "List of Formula One World Drivers' Champions") and 10 [Formula One World Constructors' Championships](/wiki/List_of_Formula_One_World_Constructors%27_Champions "List of Formula One World Constructors' Champions").

Ford is also one of only three constructors, alongside [Mercedes-Benz](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_in_motorsport "Mercedes-Benz in motorsport") and [McLaren](/wiki/McLaren "McLaren"), to complete the [Triple Crown of Motorsport](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Motorsport#Teams_and_manufacturers "Triple Crown of Motorsport") (wins at the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500"), [24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans"), and [Monaco Grand Prix](/wiki/Monaco_Grand_Prix "Monaco Grand Prix")), a feat that Ford achieved as an *engine manufacturer* by winning the [1968 Monaco Grand Prix](/wiki/1968_Monaco_Grand_Prix "1968 Monaco Grand Prix").

### Open-wheel racing

#### IndyCar

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/ArieLuyendyk1996recordcar.jpg/250px-ArieLuyendyk1996recordcar.jpg)](/wiki/File:ArieLuyendyk1996recordcar.jpg)

Arie Luyendyk's record-setting Ford Cosworth-powered IndyCar from 1996

[IndyCars](/wiki/American_open-wheel_car_racing "American open-wheel car racing") with Ford engines first competed in 1935 using a [production-based Ford V8](/wiki/Ford_flathead_V8_engine "Ford flathead V8 engine") in the Miller-Ford racer.[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203) A [pushrod](/wiki/Overhead_valve_engine "Overhead valve engine") Ford V8 raced with Lotus in 1963, and Ford's first Indy win was in 1965 with a [DOHC](/wiki/Overhead_camshaft#Dual_overhead_camshaft "Overhead camshaft") V8.[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205) Ford motors, including the Ford-sponsored [DFX engine](/wiki/Cosworth_DFV#DFX "Cosworth DFV") developed by [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth"), have won the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500") eighteen times.[[206]](#cite_note-206) On May 12, 1996, [Arie Luyendyk](/wiki/Arie_Luyendyk "Arie Luyendyk"), while driving an IndyCar powered by a Ford Cosworth XB engine, broke the [Indianapolis 500 records](/wiki/Indianapolis_500_records "Indianapolis 500 records") for fastest qualification lap (237.498 mph; 382.216 km/h) and fastest qualification four-lap average (236.986 mph; 381.392 km/h).[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208) These speed records still stand as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit).[[209]](#cite_note-209)

#### Formula Ford

[Formula Ford](/wiki/Formula_Ford "Formula Ford"), conceived in the UK in 1966, is an entry-level type of [formula racing](/wiki/Formula_racing "Formula racing") with wingless single-seater cars. Many of today's formula racing drivers started their car racing careers in this category.

#### Formula One

Main article: [Ford in Formula One](/wiki/Ford_in_Formula_One "Ford in Formula One")

See also: [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth") and [Red Bull Ford Powertrains](/wiki/Red_Bull_Ford_Powertrains "Red Bull Ford Powertrains")

Ford was heavily involved in [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") for many years and supplied engines to a large number of teams from 1967 until 2004. These engines were designed and manufactured by [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth"), the racing division that was owned by Ford from 1998 to 2004. Ford-badged engines won 176 Grands Prix between 1967 and 2003 for teams such as [Team Lotus](/wiki/Team_Lotus "Team Lotus") and [McLaren](/wiki/McLaren "McLaren"). Ford entered Formula One as a constructor in 2000 under the [Jaguar Racing](/wiki/Jaguar_Racing "Jaguar Racing") name, after buying the [Stewart Grand Prix](/wiki/Stewart_Grand_Prix "Stewart Grand Prix") team which had been its primary 'works' team in the series since 1997. Jaguar achieved little success in Formula One, and after a turbulent five seasons, Ford withdrew from the category after the [2004 season](/wiki/2004_Formula_One_season "2004 Formula One season"), selling both Jaguar Racing (which became [Red Bull Racing](/wiki/Red_Bull_Racing "Red Bull Racing")) and Cosworth (to [Gerald Forsythe](/wiki/Gerald_Forsythe "Gerald Forsythe") and [Kevin Kalkhoven](/wiki/Kevin_Kalkhoven "Kevin Kalkhoven")).[[210]](#cite_note-210)

Ford has announced that it will return to Formula One in 2026 following a partnership with [Red Bull Powertrains](/wiki/Red_Bull_Powertrains "Red Bull Powertrains"), supplying power units to Red Bull Racing and their second team [Racing Bulls](/wiki/Racing_Bulls "Racing Bulls").[[211]](#cite_note-211)

### Stock car racing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Ryan_Blaney_12_Sonoma_2023.jpg/250px-Ryan_Blaney_12_Sonoma_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ryan_Blaney_12_Sonoma_2023.jpg)

[Ryan Blaney](/wiki/Ryan_Blaney "Ryan Blaney")'s No. 12 car at [Sonoma Raceway](/wiki/Sonoma_Raceway "Sonoma Raceway") in 2023

Ford is one of three manufacturers in [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR")'s three major series: the [Cup Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Cup_Series "NASCAR Cup Series"), [Xfinity Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Xfinity_Series "NASCAR Xfinity Series"), and [Truck Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Craftsman_Truck_Series "NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series"). Major teams include [RFK Racing](/wiki/RFK_Racing "RFK Racing"), [Team Penske](/wiki/Team_Penske "Team Penske") and [Wood Brothers Racing](/wiki/Wood_Brothers_Racing "Wood Brothers Racing"). Ford is represented by the [Mustang GT](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") in the Cup Series, and the Xfinity Series and by the [F-150](/wiki/F-150 "F-150") in the Truck Series. Some of the most successful NASCAR Fords were the aerodynamic fastback [Ford Torino](/wiki/Ford_Torino "Ford Torino"), [Ford Torino Talladega](/wiki/Ford_Torino_Talladega "Ford Torino Talladega"), [Mercury Cyclone Spoiler II](/wiki/Mercury_Cyclone_Spoiler_II "Mercury Cyclone Spoiler II"), and [Mercury Montegos](/wiki/Mercury_Montego "Mercury Montego"), and the aero-era [Ford Thunderbirds](/wiki/Ford_Thunderbird "Ford Thunderbird"). The Ford nameplate has won eight [manufacturer's championships](/wiki/NASCAR_Cup_Series#Manufacturers'_Championship "NASCAR Cup Series") in the Cup Series while Mercury has won one. In the Cup Series, Ford earned its 1,000th victory in the [2013 Quicken Loans 400](/wiki/2013_Quicken_Loans_400 "2013 Quicken Loans 400") at [Michigan International Speedway](/wiki/Michigan_International_Speedway "Michigan International Speedway").[[212]](#cite_note-212) The Ford Fusion is also used in the [ARCA Menards Series](/wiki/ARCA_Menards_Series "ARCA Menards Series").

### Rally

Main article: [Ford World Rally Team](/wiki/Ford_World_Rally_Team "Ford World Rally Team")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Neste_Oil_Rally_2010_-_Jari-Matti_Latvala_in_shakedown.jpg/250px-Neste_Oil_Rally_2010_-_Jari-Matti_Latvala_in_shakedown.jpg)](/wiki/File:Neste_Oil_Rally_2010_-_Jari-Matti_Latvala_in_shakedown.jpg)

[Jari-Matti Latvala](/wiki/Jari-Matti_Latvala "Jari-Matti Latvala") driving the [Ford Focus RS WRC 09](/wiki/Ford_Focus_RS_WRC_09 "Ford Focus RS WRC 09") in 2010

Ford has a long history in [rallying](/wiki/Rallying "Rallying") and has been active in the [World Rally Championship](/wiki/World_Rally_Championship "World Rally Championship") since the beginning of the world championship, the [1973 season](/wiki/1973_World_Rally_Championship_season "1973 World Rally Championship season"). Ford took the [1979](/wiki/1979_World_Rally_Championship_season "1979 World Rally Championship season") manufacturers' title with [Hannu Mikkola](/wiki/Hannu_Mikkola "Hannu Mikkola"), [Björn Waldegård](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rn_Waldeg%C3%A5rd "Björn Waldegård"), and [Ari Vatanen](/wiki/Ari_Vatanen "Ari Vatanen") driving the [Ford Escort RS1800](/wiki/Ford_Escort_RS1800 "Ford Escort RS1800"). In the [Group B](/wiki/Group_B "Group B") era, Ford achieved success with [Ford RS200](/wiki/Ford_RS200 "Ford RS200"). Since the [1999 season](/wiki/1999_World_Rally_Championship_season "1999 World Rally Championship season"), Ford has used various versions of the [Ford Focus WRC](/wiki/Ford_Focus_WRC "Ford Focus WRC") to much success. In the [2006 season](/wiki/2006_World_Rally_Championship_season "2006 World Rally Championship season"), [BP-Ford World Rally Team](/wiki/BP-Ford_World_Rally_Team "BP-Ford World Rally Team") secured Ford its second manufacturers' title, with the Focus RS WRC 06 built by [M-Sport](/wiki/M-Sport "M-Sport") and driven by "[Flying Finns](/wiki/Flying_Finns "Flying Finns")" [Marcus Grönholm](/wiki/Marcus_Gr%C3%B6nholm "Marcus Grönholm") and [Mikko Hirvonen](/wiki/Mikko_Hirvonen "Mikko Hirvonen").[[213]](#cite_note-213) Continuing with Grönholm and Hirvonen, Ford successfully defended the manufacturers' world championship in the [2007 season](/wiki/2007_World_Rally_Championship_season "2007 World Rally Championship season"). Ford is the only manufacturer to score in the points for 92 consecutive races; since the [2002 season](/wiki/2002_World_Rally_Championship_season "2002 World Rally Championship season") opener [Monte Carlo Rally](/wiki/Monte_Carlo_Rally "Monte Carlo Rally").[[214]](#cite_note-214)

In March 1951, the Henri Loos and Henri Berney broke the cross-Africa record of the [Algiers-Cape Town Rally](/wiki/Algiers-Cape_Town_Rally "Algiers-Cape Town Rally") with a 1950 Ford V8, from Cape Town to Paris.[[215]](#cite_note-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217)

### Rallycross

Ford has competed in rallycross with its [Ford Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta") and [Ford Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus "Ford Focus"). [Tanner Foust](/wiki/Tanner_Foust "Tanner Foust") won the [Global RallyCross Championship](/wiki/Global_RallyCross_Championship "Global RallyCross Championship") in 2011 and 2012 and was runner-up in the [FIA European Rallycross Championship](/wiki/FIA_European_Rallycross_Championship "FIA European Rallycross Championship") in 2011 and 2012. [Toomas Heikkinen](/wiki/Toomas_Heikkinen "Toomas Heikkinen") won the Global RallyCross Championship title in 2013 and [Joni Wiman](/wiki/Joni_Wiman "Joni Wiman") won it in 2014. Other notable Ford drivers include [Marcus Grönholm](/wiki/Marcus_Gr%C3%B6nholm "Marcus Grönholm"), [Ken Block](/wiki/Ken_Block "Ken Block"), and [Brian Deegan](/wiki/Brian_Deegan_(rider) "Brian Deegan (rider)").

### Sports cars

Main article: [Ford GT § Racing](/wiki/Ford_GT#Racing "Ford GT")

Ford sports cars have been visible in the world of sports car racing since 1964. Most notably the [Ford GT40](/wiki/Ford_GT40 "Ford GT40") won the [24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans") four times (in [1966](/wiki/1966_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1966 24 Hours of Le Mans"), [1967](/wiki/1967_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1967 24 Hours of Le Mans"), [1968](/wiki/1968_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1968 24 Hours of Le Mans") and [1969](/wiki/1969_24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "1969 24 Hours of Le Mans")) and is the only American car to ever win overall at this prestigious event. Ford also won four titles at the [World Sportscar Championship](/wiki/World_Sportscar_Championship "World Sportscar Championship") with the GT40. Swiss team [Matech GT Racing](/wiki/Matech_Concepts "Matech Concepts"), in collaboration with Ford Racing, opened a new chapter with the [Ford GT](/wiki/Ford_GT "Ford GT"), winning the Teams title in the 2008 [FIA GT3 European Championship](/wiki/FIA_GT3_European_Championship "FIA GT3 European Championship").

#### Ford Mustang

Main article: [Ford Mustang § Racing](/wiki/Ford_Mustang#Racing "Ford Mustang")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Barbers02.jpg/250px-Barbers02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barbers02.jpg)

A GT racing version of the [Ford Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang"), competing in the [Koni Challenge](/wiki/IMSA_Continental_Tire_Sports_Car_Challenge "IMSA Continental Tire Sports Car Challenge") in 2005

The [Ford Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") has arguably been Ford's most successful sports car. [Jerry Titus](/wiki/Jerry_Titus "Jerry Titus") won the 1965 SCCA Pro B National Championship with a Mustang and the model went on to earn Ford the SCCA [Trans-Am Championship](/wiki/Trans-Am_Series "Trans-Am Series") title in both 1966 and 1967. Ford won the Trans-Am Championship again in 1970 with [Parnelli Jones](/wiki/Parnelli_Jones "Parnelli Jones") and [George Follmer](/wiki/George_Follmer "George Follmer") driving [Boss 302 Mustangs](/wiki/Boss_302_Mustang "Boss 302 Mustang") for [Bud Moore Engineering](/wiki/Bud_Moore_Engineering "Bud Moore Engineering"). Ford took the 1985 and 1986 [IMSA GTO Championship](/wiki/IMSA_GT_Championship "IMSA GT Championship") with Mustangs driven by [John Jones](/wiki/John_Jones_(racing_driver) "John Jones (racing driver)") and [Scott Pruett](/wiki/Scott_Pruett "Scott Pruett") before returning to Trans-Am glory with a championship in 1989 with [Dorsey Schroeder](/wiki/Dorsey_Schroeder "Dorsey Schroeder"). Ford dominated Trans-Am in the 1990s with [Tommy Kendall](/wiki/Tommy_Kendall "Tommy Kendall") winning championships in 1993, 1995, 1996, and 1997 with [Paul Gentilozzi](/wiki/Paul_Gentilozzi "Paul Gentilozzi") adding yet another title in 1999. In 2005 the Ford Mustang FR500C took the championship in the Rolex [Koni Challenge](/wiki/IMSA_Continental_Tire_Sports_Car_Challenge "IMSA Continental Tire Sports Car Challenge") Series in its first year on the circuit. In 2007, Ford added a victory in the GT4 European Championship. 2008 was the first year of the Mustang Challenge for the Miller Cup, a series that pits a full field of identical factory-built Ford Mustang race cars against each other. Also, in 2008, Ford won the manufacturers championship in the [Koni Challenge](/wiki/IMSA_Continental_Tire_Sports_Car_Challenge "IMSA Continental Tire Sports Car Challenge") Series and HyperSport drivers Joe Foster and Steve Maxwell won the drivers title in a Mustang GT.

Ford and [Michelin](/wiki/Michelin "Michelin") teamed up to provide custom-engineered tires for the [Ford Performance](/wiki/Ford_Performance "Ford Performance") vehicle lineup. [Ford Performance](/wiki/Ford_Performance "Ford Performance") director Dave Pericak said: "That confidence extends from our upcoming racing effort at Le Mans in 2016 with the all-new [Ford GT](/wiki/Ford_GT "Ford GT"), to the Ford Performance vehicle lineup, including the [Shelby GT350](/wiki/Shelby_GT350 "Shelby GT350") and F-150 Raptor".[[218]](#cite_note-218)

### Touring cars

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Winterbottom2008.jpg/250px-Winterbottom2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winterbottom2008.jpg)

[Ford Performance Racing](/wiki/Ford_Performance_Racing "Ford Performance Racing") [Ford Falcon](/wiki/Ford_Falcon_(Australia) "Ford Falcon (Australia)") [V8 Supercar](/wiki/V8_Supercar "V8 Supercar") at Eastern Creek in Australia in 2008

Ford has campaigned touring cars such as the [Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang"), [Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus_(international) "Ford Focus (international)"), [Falcon](/wiki/Ford_Falcon_(Australia) "Ford Falcon (Australia)"), and [Contour](/wiki/Ford_Contour "Ford Contour")/[Mondeo](/wiki/Ford_Mondeo "Ford Mondeo") and the [Sierra](/wiki/Ford_Sierra "Ford Sierra") [Cosworth](/wiki/Cosworth "Cosworth") in many different series throughout the years. Notably, Mondeo drivers finished 1, 2, and 3 in the 2000 [British Touring Car Championship](/wiki/British_Touring_Car_Championship "British Touring Car Championship") and Falcon drivers placed 1, 2, and 3 in the [2005 V8 Supercar Championship Series](/wiki/2005_V8_Supercar_Championship_Series "2005 V8 Supercar Championship Series").

### Drag racing

In [drag racing](/wiki/Drag_racing "Drag racing"), [John Force Racing](/wiki/John_Force_Racing "John Force Racing") drivers [John Force](/wiki/John_Force "John Force"), [Tony Pedregon](/wiki/Tony_Pedregon "Tony Pedregon"), and [Robert Hight](/wiki/Robert_Hight "Robert Hight") have piloted [Ford Mustang](/wiki/Ford_Mustang "Ford Mustang") [Funny Cars](/wiki/Funny_Car "Funny Car") to several [NHRA](/wiki/National_Hot_Rod_Association "National Hot Rod Association") titles in recent seasons. Teammates [Tim Wilkerson](/wiki/Tim_Wilkerson "Tim Wilkerson") and Bob Tasca III also drive Mustangs in Funny Car.

### Drifting

Ford has branched out into [drifting](/wiki/Drifting_(motorsport) "Drifting (motorsport)") with the introduction of the new model Mustang. Most noticeable is the Turquoise and Blue Falken Tires Mustang driven by [Vaughn Gittin, Jr.](/wiki/Vaughn_Gittin,_Jr. "Vaughn Gittin, Jr.") that produces 750 hp (560 kW).

## Environmental initiatives

### Flexible fuel vehicles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Ford_Focus_Flexifuel_in_Madrid_with_flexifuel_badging.jpg/250px-Ford_Focus_Flexifuel_in_Madrid_with_flexifuel_badging.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Focus_Flexifuel_in_Madrid_with_flexifuel_badging.jpg)

The Ford Focus Flexifuel was the first [E85](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures#E85 "Common ethanol fuel mixtures") [flexible fuel vehicle](/wiki/Flexible_fuel_vehicle "Flexible fuel vehicle") commercially available in the European market.

[Flexible fuel vehicles](/wiki/Flexible_fuel_vehicle "Flexible fuel vehicle") are designed to operate smoothly using a wide range of available [ethanol fuel](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") mixtures—from pure gasoline to [bioethanol](/wiki/Bioethanol "Bioethanol")-gasoline blends such as [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85") (85% [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol_fuel "Ethanol fuel") and 15% gasoline) or [E100](/wiki/Common_ethanol_fuel_mixtures "Common ethanol fuel mixtures") (neat [hydrous](/wiki/Hydrous "Hydrous") ethanol) in Brazil. Part of the challenge of successful marketing alternative and flexible fuel vehicles in the U.S. is the general lack of establishment of sufficient [fueling stations](/wiki/Fueling_station "Fueling station"), which would be essential for these vehicles to be attractive to a wide range of consumers. Significant efforts to ramp up production and distribution of [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85") fuels are underway and expanding.[[219]](#cite_note-219)
