#### Hybrid electric vehicles

See also: [Hybrid electric vehicle](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Ford_Escape_plug-in_hybrid.jpg/250px-Ford_Escape_plug-in_hybrid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Escape_plug-in_hybrid.jpg)

[Ford Escape](/wiki/Ford_Escape "Ford Escape") [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") test vehicle

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/George_Bush_visit_Kansas_City_Assembly.jpg/250px-George_Bush_visit_Kansas_City_Assembly.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Bush_visit_Kansas_City_Assembly.jpg)

[Alan Mulally](/wiki/Alan_Mulally "Alan Mulally") (wearing red tie) with then-President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") at the [Kansas City Assembly](/wiki/Kansas_City_Assembly "Kansas City Assembly") plant in [Claycomo](/wiki/Claycomo,_Missouri "Claycomo, Missouri"), Missouri, on March 20, 2007, touting Ford's new hybrid cars

In 2004, Ford and Toyota agreed a patent-sharing accord that granted Ford access to certain hybrid technology patented by Toyota; in exchange, Ford licensed some of its own patents to Toyota.[[220]](#cite_note-220)[[221]](#cite_note-221)[[222]](#cite_note-222) In 2004, Ford introduced the [Escape Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_Escape_Hybrid "Ford Escape Hybrid"). With this vehicle, Ford was third to the automotive market with a [hybrid electric vehicle](/wiki/Hybrid_vehicle "Hybrid vehicle") and the first hybrid electric [SUV](/wiki/Sport_utility_vehicle "Sport utility vehicle") to market. This was also the first hybrid electric vehicle with a [flexible fuel](/wiki/Flexible_fuel "Flexible fuel") capability to run on [E85](/wiki/E85 "E85").[[223]](#cite_note-223) The Escape's platform mate [Mercury Mariner](/wiki/Mercury_Mariner "Mercury Mariner") was also available with the hybrid-electric system in the 2006 model year—a full year ahead of schedule. The similar [Mazda Tribute](/wiki/Mazda_Tribute "Mazda Tribute") will also receive a hybrid-electric powertrain option, along with many other vehicles in the Ford vehicle line.

In 2005, Ford announced a goal to make 250,000 hybrids a year by 2010, but by mid-2006 announced that it would not meet that goal, due to excessively high costs and the lack of sufficient supplies of the hybrid-electric batteries and drivetrain system components.[[224]](#cite_note-224) Instead, Ford has committed to accelerating development of next-generation hybrid-electric power plants in Britain, in collaboration with Volvo. This engineering study is expected to yield more than 100 new hybrid-electric vehicle models and derivatives.

In September 2007, Ford announced a partnership with [Southern California Edison](/wiki/Southern_California_Edison "Southern California Edison") (SCE) to examine how [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") will work with the electrical grid. Under the multimillion-dollar, multi-year project, Ford will convert a demonstration fleet of [Ford Escape Hybrids](/wiki/Ford_Escape_Hybrid "Ford Escape Hybrid") into plug-in hybrids, and SCE will evaluate how the vehicles might interact with the home and the utility's electrical grid. Some of the vehicles will be evaluated "in typical customer settings", according to Ford.[[40]](#cite_note-akhrum-40)[[41]](#cite_note-edison-41)

On June 12, 2008, [USDOE](/wiki/USDOE "USDOE") expanded its own fleet of alternative fuel and advanced technology vehicles with the addition of a Ford Escape Plug-In Hybrid Flex-Fuel Vehicle. The vehicle is equipped with a 10-kilowatt (13 hp) [lithium-ion](/wiki/Lithium-ion "Lithium-ion") battery supplied by [Johnson Controls-Saft](/wiki/Johnson_Controls-Saft "Johnson Controls-Saft") that stores enough electric energy to drive up to 30 miles (48 km) at speeds of up to 40 mph (64 km/h).[[225]](#cite_note-225) In March 2009, Ford launched hybrid versions of the [Ford Fusion Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_Fusion_Hybrid "Ford Fusion Hybrid") and the [Mercury Milan Hybrid](/wiki/Mercury_Milan_Hybrid "Mercury Milan Hybrid") in the United States, both as 2010 models.[[226]](#cite_note-GreenCar0409-226)

As of November 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), Ford has produced for retail sales the following hybrid electric vehicles: [Ford Escape Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_Escape_Hybrid "Ford Escape Hybrid") (2004–2012), [Mercury Mariner Hybrid](/wiki/Mercury_Mariner#Hybrid "Mercury Mariner") (2005–2010), [Mercury Milan Hybrid](/wiki/Mercury_Milan_Hybrid "Mercury Milan Hybrid") (2009–2010), [Ford Fusion Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_Fusion_Hybrid "Ford Fusion Hybrid") (2009–present), [Lincoln MKZ Hybrid](/wiki/Lincoln_MKZ_Hybrid "Lincoln MKZ Hybrid") (2010–present), [Ford C-Max Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_C-Max_Hybrid "Ford C-Max Hybrid") (2012–present), and [Ford Mondeo Hybrid](/wiki/Ford_Mondeo_Hybrid "Ford Mondeo Hybrid") (2014–present). By June 2012, Ford had sold 200,000 full hybrids in the U.S. since 2004,[[227]](#cite_note-Ford200K-227) and, as of September 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), the carmaker has sold over 344,000 hybrids in the United States.[[227]](#cite_note-Ford200K-227)[[228]](#cite_note-Sales2012US-228)[[229]](#cite_note-Sales2013US-229)[[230]](#cite_note-Sales092014US-230) The top selling hybrids in the U.S. market are the Fusion Hybrid with 127,572 units, followed by Escape Hybrid with 117,997 units, and the C-Max Hybrid with 54,236.[[228]](#cite_note-Sales2012US-228)[[229]](#cite_note-Sales2013US-229)[[230]](#cite_note-Sales092014US-230)[[231]](#cite_note-Sales2011US-231)[[232]](#cite_note-DoEHEV10-232) As of November 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), Ford is the world's second-largest manufacturer of hybrids after [Toyota Motor Corporation](/wiki/Toyota_Motor_Corporation "Toyota Motor Corporation"), with 400,000 hybrid electric vehicles produced since their introduction in 2004.[[233]](#cite_note-233)

#### Plug-in electric vehicles

See also: [Plug-in electric vehicle](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicle "Plug-in electric vehicle")

As of April 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ford_Motor_Company&action=edit), Ford currently produces the following [plug-in electric vehicles](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicle "Plug-in electric vehicle"): the [Ford Escape](/wiki/Ford_Escape#Fourth_generation_(2020) "Ford Escape")/[Kuga PHEV](/wiki/Ford_Kuga#Third_generation_(CX482;_2019) "Ford Kuga"), [Ford Ranger PHEV](/wiki/Ford_Ranger_(T6)#P703 "Ford Ranger (T6)"), [Ford Mustang Mach-E](/wiki/Ford_Mustang_Mach-E "Ford Mustang Mach-E"), [Ford F-150 Lightning](/wiki/Ford_F-150_Lightning "Ford F-150 Lightning"), [Ford Explorer EV](/wiki/Ford_Explorer_EV "Ford Explorer EV") (Europe), [Ford E-Transit/Tourneo Courier](/wiki/Ford_Transit_Courier "Ford Transit Courier"), [Ford E-Transit Custom](/wiki/Ford_E-Transit_Custom "Ford E-Transit Custom") and the [Ford E-Transit](/wiki/Ford_E-Transit "Ford E-Transit").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Transit_Connect_EV_EDTA_DC_04_2011_1806.jpg/250px-Transit_Connect_EV_EDTA_DC_04_2011_1806.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transit_Connect_EV_EDTA_DC_04_2011_1806.jpg)

The [Azure Transit Connect Electric](/wiki/Azure_Transit_Connect_Electric "Azure Transit Connect Electric") was produced between 2010 and 2012 as a collaboration between [Azure Dynamics](/wiki/Azure_Dynamics "Azure Dynamics") and Ford Motor Company.

Bill Ford was one of the first top industry executives to make regular use of a [battery electric vehicle](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle"), a [Ford Ranger EV](/wiki/Ford_Ranger_EV "Ford Ranger EV"), while the company contracted with the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") to deliver electric postal vans based on the Ranger EV platform. Ford discontinued a line of electric [Ranger](/wiki/Ford_Ranger "Ford Ranger") [pickup trucks](/wiki/Pickup_truck "Pickup truck") and ordered them destroyed, though it reversed in January 2005, after environmentalist protest.[[234]](#cite_note-234) The all-electric pickup truck leased 205 units to individuals and 1,500 units to fleets in the U.S. from 1998 to 2002.[[235]](#cite_note-Rand02-235)[[236]](#cite_note-Boschert06-236)

From 2009 to 2011, Ford offered the [Ford TH!NK](/wiki/Ford_TH!NK "Ford TH!NK") car. Ford ended production and ordered all the cars repossessed and destroyed, even as many of the people leasing them begged to be able to buy the cars from Ford. After an outcry from the lessees and activists in the U.S. and Norway, Ford returned the cars to Norway for sale.[[237]](#cite_note-237) Four hundred and forty units were leased in the U.S. from 1999 until 2003.[[236]](#cite_note-Boschert06-236)

In 2017, Ford CEO Mark Fields announced that the company would invest $4.5 billion in further development of plug-in electric vehicles by 2020.[[238]](#cite_note-238)

The 2010–2012 [Azure Transit Connect Electric](/wiki/Azure_Transit_Connect_Electric "Azure Transit Connect Electric") is an [all-electric](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") [van](/wiki/Van "Van") that was developed as a collaboration between [Azure Dynamics](/wiki/Azure_Dynamics "Azure Dynamics") and Ford Motor Company, but Azure was the official manufacturer of record.[[239]](#cite_note-WSJ0910-239)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Ford_Fusion_Energi_SEL_with_badge_WAS_2012_0583.jpg/250px-Ford_Fusion_Energi_SEL_with_badge_WAS_2012_0583.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Fusion_Energi_SEL_with_badge_WAS_2012_0583.jpg)

The [Ford Fusion Energi](/wiki/Ford_Fusion_Energi "Ford Fusion Energi") [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") shares its powertrain with the [Ford C-Max Energi](/wiki/Ford_C-Max_Energi "Ford C-Max Energi").

The 2011–2018 [Ford Focus Electric](/wiki/Ford_Focus_Electric "Ford Focus Electric") is based on the third-generation [Focus](/wiki/Ford_Focus_(North_America) "Ford Focus (North America)") internal combustion vehicle, converted to an [all-electric](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") propulsion system as a production [electric car](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car") by [Magna International](/wiki/Magna_International "Magna International"), and retail sales began in the U.S. in December 2011.[[240]](#cite_note-240)[[241]](#cite_note-241)

The 2012–2017 [Ford C-Max Energi](/wiki/Ford_C-Max_Energi "Ford C-Max Energi") is a [plug-in hybrid](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid") released in the U.S. in October 2012.[[242]](#cite_note-Energi2013_14-242) It is followed by the [Ford Fusion Energi](/wiki/Ford_Fusion_Energi "Ford Fusion Energi") that was produced in 2013–2020.[[243]](#cite_note-EnergiDelivery01-243)[[244]](#cite_note-EnergiDelivery02-244) Both Energi models share the same powertrain technology.[[242]](#cite_note-Energi2013_14-242)

In October 2017, Ford announced its Team Edison battery electric vehicle group to lead the company's renewed efforts into the EV market, which had plans for a small 300-mile range SUV by 2020.[[245]](#cite_note-245) The new team will be headquartered in Detroit and have offices in Europe and Asia.[[246]](#cite_note-246)

[![a photograph showing the front-left quarter of a grey automobile](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/2021_Ford_Mustang_Mach-E_Premium%2C_front_left%2C_12-17-2022.jpg/250px-2021_Ford_Mustang_Mach-E_Premium%2C_front_left%2C_12-17-2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021_Ford_Mustang_Mach-E_Premium,_front_left,_12-17-2022.jpg)

[Ford Mustang Mach-E](/wiki/Ford_Mustang_Mach-E "Ford Mustang Mach-E")

On November 17, 2019, the [Mustang Mach-E](/wiki/Ford_Mustang_Mach-E "Ford Mustang Mach-E") was introduced which later went on sale December 2020 as a 2021 model.[[247]](#cite_note-247)[[248]](#cite_note-248) The Mustang Mach-E is assembled at [Cuautitlán Assembly](/wiki/Cuautitl%C3%A1n_Assembly "Cuautitlán Assembly") in [Cuautitlán Izcalli](/wiki/Cuautitl%C3%A1n_Izcalli "Cuautitlán Izcalli"), Mexico.[[249]](#cite_note-forbes1-249) According to former Ford CEO Jim Hackett, assembling the vehicle in Mexico allows Ford to make a profit from the first vehicle, unlike other electric vehicles. In June 2022, the CFO of Ford announced that the Mustang Mach-E was no longer profitable due to increases in the cost of raw materials.[[250]](#cite_note-250)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/President_Biden_Ford_F150_Lightning_05_20_2021.jpg/250px-President_Biden_Ford_F150_Lightning_05_20_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Biden_Ford_F150_Lightning_05_20_2021.jpg)

President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") test driving the [F-150 Lightning](/wiki/Ford_F-150_Lightning "Ford F-150 Lightning") prototype at Ford's Rouge Electric Vehicle Center, Dearborn, Michigan.

On May 19, 2021, Ford revealed a new electric pickup truck, the [F-150 Lightning](/wiki/Ford_F-150_Lightning "Ford F-150 Lightning").[[251]](#cite_note-cnbc20210521-251) The first F-150 Lightning was manufactured on April 18, 2022, with first delivery in the US on May 26.[[252]](#cite_note-252) Ford adjusted its 2024 production plans for the F-150 Lightning, cutting them in half from an anticipated 3,200 weekly units to around 1,600 weekly units due to sales below expectations.[[253]](#cite_note-253) In 2022, Ford began manufacturing its E-Transit electric cargo vans at its plant in Kansas City, Missouri.[[254]](#cite_note-254)

As the result of 2019 Ford-VW global alliance cooperation agreement,[[255]](#cite_note-255) Ford began developing electric vehicles for the European market using the [Volkswagen Group MEB platform](/wiki/Volkswagen_Group_MEB_platform "Volkswagen Group MEB platform") along with batteries supplied by Volkswagen. The first Ford product based on the MEB is the [Ford Explorer EV](/wiki/Ford_Explorer_EV "Ford Explorer EV"), which was introduced in March 2023. It is produced in the [Cologne plant](/wiki/Cologne_Body_%26_Assembly "Cologne Body & Assembly") in Germany, which previously manufactured the [Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta") small car.[[256]](#cite_note-:2-256) In August 2023, Ford delayed the Explorer EV deliveries to 2024 due to new battery regulations.[[257]](#cite_note-257)

In March 2022, Ford increased its focus in battery electric vehicles by establishing Ford Model E, a division for Ford's electric vehicle business. Ford Model E is expected to be profitable by 2026, and the company said the division "should be seen as a [startup](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company")".[[84]](#cite_note-:0-84)[[4]](#cite_note-DFP0302-4)[[88]](#cite_note-:1-88) In June 2022, Ford announced its intention to restructure its dealership model, including building an [e-commerce](/wiki/E-commerce "E-commerce") platform where customers can buy electric vehicles at non-negotiable prices in an effort to match [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla,_Inc. "Tesla, Inc.")"s profit margins.[[258]](#cite_note-tc20220602-258) Ford also stated in June 2022 that it planned to spend $3.7 billion to hire 6,200 union workers to staff several assembly plants in Michigan, Ohio and Missouri in a bid to sell 2 million electric vehicles annually by 2026.[[258]](#cite_note-tc20220602-258)

In May 2023, Ford announced its plans to integrate the [North American Charging System](/wiki/North_American_Charging_System "North American Charging System") (NACS) system into their electric vehicles, following Tesla's decision to [open](/wiki/Open_standard "Open standard") and rename its proprietary charging standard to NACS. Newly built Ford electric vehicles after 2024 will have native NACS charging ports on the vehicle. Existing Ford electric models will be able to connect to the NACS system and its chargers by use of an adapter. Both will thus have access to the extensive NACS [charging network](/wiki/Electric_vehicle_network "Electric vehicle network") with more than 12,000 chargers in North America.[[259]](#cite_note-ars20230525-259)

#### 2025 investment

In August 2025, Ford announced that it was investing almost $5 billion to produce lower-cost EV products. $2 billion will be directed towards its [Louisville](/wiki/Louisville,_Kentucky "Louisville, Kentucky") factory to develop lower-cost electric vehicle components as well as a new "assembly tree" manufacturing process. Rather than one long conveyor, three subassembly lines will operate simultaneously and then converge together. The first vehicle from the Louisville factory is set to launch in 2027 and will be a medium, four-door pickup truck at a starting price of $30,000. The Louisville location will also stop production of the [Ford Escape](/wiki/Ford_Escape "Ford Escape") and [Lincoln Corsair](/wiki/Lincoln_Corsair "Lincoln Corsair") by 2026.[[260]](#cite_note-260)[[261]](#cite_note-Boudette-261) Meanwhile, $3 billion is intended to build a new battery plant in Michigan.[[262]](#cite_note-262)

The announcement follows Ford's electric vehicle division losing $12 billion over the last two and a half years, including $2.2 billion in the first half of 2025.[[261]](#cite_note-Boudette-261)

### Hydrogen

Ford also continues to study [fuel cell](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell")-powered electric powertrains and has demonstrated hydrogen-fueled [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine") technologies, as well as developing the next-generation hybrid-electric systems. Compared with conventional vehicles, [hybrid vehicles](/wiki/Hybrid_vehicle "Hybrid vehicle") and fuel cell vehicles decrease air pollution emissions as well as sound levels with favorable impacts upon respiratory health and decrease of [noise health effects](/wiki/Noise_health_effects "Noise health effects").

Ford has launched the production of [hydrogen-powered](/wiki/Hydrogen_vehicle "Hydrogen vehicle") shuttle buses, using hydrogen instead of gasoline in a standard [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Hydrogen_vehicle#Internal_combustion_vehicle "Hydrogen vehicle"), for use at airports and convention centers.[[263]](#cite_note-263) At the 2006 [Greater Los Angeles Auto Show](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles_Auto_Show "Greater Los Angeles Auto Show"), Ford showcased a hydrogen fuel cell version of its Explorer SUV. The Fuel cell Explorer has a combined output of 174 hp (130 kW). It has a large hydrogen storage tank which is situated in the center of the car taking the original place of the conventional model's automatic transmission. The centered position of the tank assists the vehicle reach a notable range of 350 miles (563 km), the farthest for a fuel cell vehicle so far. The fuel cell Explorer the first in a series of prototypes partly funded by the [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") to expand efforts to determine the feasibility of hydrogen-powered vehicles. The fuel cell Explorer is one of several vehicles with green technology being featured at the L.A. show, including the 2008 Ford Escape Hybrid, PZEV emissions compliant Fusion and Focus models and a 2008 Ford F-Series Super Duty outfitted with Ford's clean diesel technology.

### Increased fuel efficiency

In July 2008, Ford Motor Company announced that it would accelerate its plans to produce more fuel-efficient cars, changing both its North American manufacturing plans and its lineup of vehicles available in the United States. In terms of North American manufacturing, the company planned to convert three existing pickup truck and sport utility vehicle ([SUV](/wiki/SUV "SUV")) plants for small car production, with the first conversion at its Michigan Truck Plant. In addition, Ford's assembly plants near Mexico City, Mexico, and in Louisville, Kentucky, were to be converted from pickups and SUVs to small cars, including the Ford Fiesta, by 2011. Ford then also planned to introduce to North America six of its European small vehicles, including two versions of the Ford Fiesta, by the end of 2012.[[264]](#cite_note-264)

Ford of Europe developed the [ECOnetic](/wiki/ECOnetic "ECOnetic") programme to address the market and legislative need for higher [fuel efficiency](/wiki/Fuel_efficiency "Fuel efficiency") and lower CO2 emissions. As opposed to the [hybrid engine](/wiki/Hybrid_engine "Hybrid engine") technology used in competitor products such as the [Toyota Prius](/wiki/Toyota_Prius "Toyota Prius"), ECOnetic improves existing technology. Using lower-consuming [Duratorq TDCi](/wiki/Ford_Duratorq_engine "Ford Duratorq engine") diesel engines, and based on a combination of improved aerodynamics, lower resistance, and improved efficiency, the [Ford Fiesta](/wiki/Ford_Fiesta "Ford Fiesta") was the lowest-emitting mass-produced car in Europe[[265]](#cite_note-265) while the [2012 Ford Focus ECOnetic](/wiki/Ford_Focus_(international) "Ford Focus (international)") will have better fuel consumption than the Prius or the [Volkswagen Golf](/wiki/Volkswagen_Golf "Volkswagen Golf") [BlueMotion](/wiki/BlueMotion "BlueMotion").[[266]](#cite_note-266) ECOnetic is not presently planned to be sold in North American due to current perceived lower consumer demand.[[267]](#cite_note-267)

Ford has challenged University teams to create a vehicle that is simple, durable, lightweight, and comes equipped with a base target price of only $7,000. The students from [Aachen University](/wiki/Aachen_University "Aachen University") created the "2015 Ford Model T".[[268]](#cite_note-268)[[269]](#cite_note-269)

In 2000, under the leadership of the current Ford chairman, William Clay Ford, the company announced[[270]](#cite_note-270) a planned 25 percent improvement in the average mileage of its SUVs—to be completed by the 2005 [calendar year](/wiki/Calendar_year "Calendar year"). In 2003, Ford announced that competitive market conditions and technological and cost challenges would prevent the company from achieving this goal.[[271]](#cite_note-271)

For the 2007 model year, Ford had thirteen U.S. models that achieve 30 miles per gallon or better (based on the highway fuel economy estimates of the EPA) and several of Ford's vehicles were recognized in the EPA and Department of Energy Fuel Economy Guide for best-in-class fuel economy. Ford claimed to have eliminated nearly three million pounds of smog-forming emissions from their U.S. cars and light trucks over the 2004 to 2006 model years.[[272]](#cite_note-272) However, the United States Environmental Protection Agency has linked Ford to 54 [Superfund](/wiki/Superfund "Superfund") toxic waste sites, twelve of which have been cleaned up and deleted from the list.[[273]](#cite_note-273)

### Efficient buildings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Ford_Rouge_Plant_green_roof_2019.jpg/250px-Ford_Rouge_Plant_green_roof_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Rouge_Plant_green_roof_2019.jpg)

[Green roof](/wiki/Green_roof "Green roof") that covers part of the [Ford River Rouge Complex](/wiki/Ford_River_Rouge_Complex "Ford River Rouge Complex").

As part of a renovation of the [Ford River Rouge Complex](/wiki/Ford_River_Rouge_Complex "Ford River Rouge Complex"), in 2010 Ford unveiled a 10.4-acre (42,000 m2) living roof covering part of the Dearborn Truck plant, consisting of [sedum](/wiki/Sedum "Sedum"), a low-growing groundcover. The sedum retains and cleanses rainwater and moderates the internal temperature of the building, saving energy.[[274]](#cite_note-274)

In 2016, Ford announced a ten-year renovation plan for its Dearborn campus.[[275]](#cite_note-275) The plan features consolidation of office and lab spaces in to fewer and much larger buildings, which will be built to [LEED](/wiki/LEED "LEED") standards, and will feature extensive use of wetlands and park spaces.[[276]](#cite_note-276)[[277]](#cite_note-277) The new campus features new multi-story parking decks with solar power-generating roofs,[[278]](#cite_note-278) and a new natural gas power plant. The DTE Ford Central Energy Plant is a 34MW combined heat-and-power plant which features a high efficiency design and LEED Gold buildings.[[279]](#cite_note-279)

## Sponsorships

Ford sponsors numerous events and sports facilities around the U.S., most notably the [Ford Center](/wiki/Ford_Center_(Evansville) "Ford Center (Evansville)") in downtown [Evansville, Indiana](/wiki/Evansville,_Indiana "Evansville, Indiana"), and [Ford Field](/wiki/Ford_Field "Ford Field") in [downtown Detroit](/wiki/Downtown_Detroit "Downtown Detroit").[[280]](#cite_note-280)

The company has also been a major sponsor of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") for over two decades, and is also a longtime sponsor of the [Sky media channel's](/wiki/Sky_plc "Sky plc") coverage of [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") football.

Between 1994 and 1999, Ford was the main kit sponsor of German [Bundesliga](/wiki/Bundesliga "Bundesliga") club [1. FC Köln](/wiki/1._FC_K%C3%B6ln "1. FC Köln").

## Further reading

* Bak, Richard. *Henry and Edsel: The Creation of the Ford Empire* (2003)
* Bardou; Jean-Pierre, Jean-Jacques Chanaron, Patrick Fridenson, and James M. Laux. *The Automobile Revolution: The Impact of an Industry* University of North Carolina Press, 1982
* Batchelor, Ray. *Henry Ford: Mass Production, Modernism and Design* Manchester U. Press, 1994
* Bonin, Huber et al. *Ford, 1902–2003: The European History* 2 vol Paris 2003. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [2-914369-06-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/2-914369-06-9 "Special:BookSources/2-914369-06-9") scholarly essays in English on Ford operations in Europe; reviewed in Len Holden, Len. "Fording the Atlantic: Ford and Fordism in Europe" in *Business History*  Volume 47, #January 1, 2005, pp 122–127
* [Bowman, Timothy J. *Spirituality at Work: An Exploratory Sociological Investigation of the Ford Motor Company*. London School of Economics and Political Science, 2004](https://web.archive.org/web/20110728045240/http://www.spiritatwork.org/knowledgecenter/dissertations/bowman_tim_spirituality_at_work.pdf)
* Brinkley, Douglas G. *Wheels for the World: Henry Ford, His Company, and a Century of Progress* (2003)
* Brinkley, Douglas. "Prime Mover". *American Heritage* 2003 54(3): 44–53. on Model T
* Bryan, Ford R. *Henry's Lieutenants*, 1993; [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-8143-2428-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-8143-2428-2 "Special:BookSources/0-8143-2428-2")
* Bucci, Federico. *Albert Kahn: Architect of Ford* Princeton Architectural Press, 1993
* Cabadas, Joseph P. *River Rouge: Ford's Industrial Colossus* (2004), heavily illustrated
* Dempsey, Mary A. "Fordlandia' *Michigan History* 1994 78(4): 24–33. Ford's rubber plantation in Brazil
* Flink, James. *America Adopts the Automobile, 1895–1910* MIT Press, 1970
* Foster, Mark S. "The Model T, The Hard Sell, and Los Angeles Urban Growth: The Decentralization of Los Angeles During the 1920s." *Pacific Historical Review* 44.4 (November 1975): 459–84
* Halberstam, David. *[The Reckoning](/wiki/The_Reckoning_(Halberstam_book) "The Reckoning (Halberstam book)")* (1986) detailed reporting on decline of the auto industry. [online](https://archive.org/details/reckoning00halb); also
* [Iacocca, Lee](/wiki/Lee_Iacocca "Lee Iacocca") and [William Novak](/wiki/William_Novak "William Novak"). *[Iacocca: An Autobiography](/wiki/Iacocca:_An_Autobiography "Iacocca: An Autobiography")* (1984)
* Jacobson, D. S. "The Political Economy of Industrial Location: the Ford Motor Company at Cork 1912–26." Irish Economic and Social History [Ireland] 1977 4: 36–55. Ford and Irish politics
* Lacey, Robert "Ford: The Men and the Machine" (Heinnemann, London) 0 414 401027 (1986)
* Levinson, William A. *Henry Ford's Lean Vision: Enduring Principles from the First Ford Motor Plant*, 2002; [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [1-56327-260-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/1-56327-260-1 "Special:BookSources/1-56327-260-1")
* Kuhn, Arthur J. *GM Passes Ford, 1918–1938: Designing the General Motors Performance-Control System.* Pennsylvania State University Press, 1986
* Magee, David. *Ford Tough: Bill Ford and the Battle to Rebuild America's Automaker* (2004)
* [Maxton, Graeme P.](/wiki/Graeme_Maxton "Graeme Maxton") and John Wormald, *Time for a Model Change: Re-engineering the Global Automotive Industry* (2004)
* May, George S. *A Most Unique Machine: The Michigan Origins of the American Automobile Industry* Eerdman's, 1975
* Maynard, Micheline. *The End of Detroit: How the Big Three Lost Their Grip on the American Car Market* (2003)
* McIntyre, Stephen L. "The Failure of Fordism: Reform of the Automobile Repair Industry, 1913–1940: *Technology and Culture* 2000 41(2): 269–299. repair shops rejected flat rates
* Nevins, Allan. *Ford: the Times, the Man, the Company* (vol 1 1954) [online](https://archive.org/details/in.ernet.dli.2015.547601)
* Nevins, Allan, and Frank Hill. *Ford: Expansion and Challenge 1915-1933* (vol 2, 1957) [online](https://archive.org/details/fordexpansioncha00alla)
* Nevins, Allan. *Ford: Decline and rebirth, 1933-1962* (vol 3, 1963) [online](https://archive.org/details/forddeclinerebir00nevi)
* [Rubenstein, James M.](/wiki/James_M._Rubenstein "James M. Rubenstein") *The Changing U.S. Auto Industry: A Geographical Analysis*, [Routledge](/wiki/Routledge "Routledge"), 1992
* Shiomi, Haruhito and Kazuo Wada. *Fordism Transformed: The Development of Production Methods in the Automobile Industry*, [Oxford University Press](/wiki/Oxford_University_Press "Oxford University Press"), 1995
* [Sorensen, Charles E.](/wiki/Charles_E._Sorensen "Charles E. Sorensen") (1956), [*My Forty Years with Ford*](https://babel.hathitrust.org/cgi/pt?id=mdp.39015004575919&seq=11), New York: W. W. Norton, [LCCN](/wiki/LCCN_(identifier) "LCCN (identifier)") [56010854](https://lccn.loc.gov/56010854), [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [912748](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/912748). Various republications, including [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780814332795](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780814332795 "Special:BookSources/9780814332795").
* Studer-Noguez; Isabel. *Ford and the Global Strategies of Multinationals: The North American Auto Industry* Routledge, 2002
* Tedlow, Richard S. "The Struggle for Dominance in the Automobile Market: the Early Years of Ford and General Motors" *Business and Economic History* 1988 17: 49–62. Ford stressed low price based on efficient factories, but GM did better in oligopolistic competition by including investment in manufacturing, marketing, and management
* Thomas, Robert Paul. "The Automobile Industry and its Tycoon" *Explorations in Entrepreneurial History* 1969 6(2): 139–157. argues Ford did NOT have much influence on US industry
* Watts, Steven. *The People's Tycoon: Henry Ford and the American Century* (2005)
* Wik, Reynold M. *Henry Ford and Grass-Roots America*, [University of Michigan Press](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Press "University of Michigan Press"), 1972. impact on farmers
* [Wilkins, Mira](/wiki/Mira_Wilkins "Mira Wilkins") and Frank Ernest Hill, *American Business Abroad: Ford on Six Continents* Wayne State University Press, 1964
* Williams, Karel, Colin Haslam and John Williams, "Ford versus 'Fordism': The Beginning of Mass Production?" *Work, Employment & Society*, Vol. 6, No. 4, 517–555 (1992), stress on Ford's flexibility and commitment to continuous improvements.
